topics.title: GD with Momentum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT745 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** GD with Momentum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gradient Descent with Momentum (GD-M)** is a first-order, stochastic-variants-compatible optimization algorithm that augments the standard gradient descent update rule by maintaining an exponentially decaying moving average (the **velocity vector**) of past gradients. It is formally designed to accelerate convergence in regions of high curvature, suppress oscillations across ravines, and improve traversal through shallow local minima in the loss landscape.

In the canonical formulation attributed to Polyak (1964), given an objective (loss) function $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ parameterized by weights $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, the iterative updates are:

$$
v_{t+1} \;=\; \beta \, v_{t} \;+\; \eta \, \nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})
$$

$$
\theta_{t+1} \;=\; \theta_{t} \;-\; v_{t+1}
$$

where:
- $\eta > 0$ is the **learning rate**
- $\beta \in [0,1)$ is the **momentum coefficient** (hyperparameter), typically set to **0.9**
- $v_{t} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the **velocity vector** (initialized as the zero vector)
- $\nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})$ is the gradient at iteration $t$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine rolling a heavy ball down a hill covered in fog.**
> - In **vanilla GD**, the ball only feels the slope *at its current footstep*. Every step is independent — if the slope zig-zags, the ball zig-zags.
> - With **momentum**, the ball has *physical mass*. As it rolls, it accumulates velocity in directions where the slope has been consistently downhill. Tiny zig-zag bumps (noisy gradients) cancel out because past opposing motions are remembered; meanwhile, the consistent downhill direction builds up speed.

This is why momentum helps in two real problems:
1. **Ravines / Saddle Points**: The loss surface of a deep network has long, narrow valleys. Vanilla GD oscillates sideways while creeping forward. Momentum averages out the sideways oscillation and amplifies the consistent forward direction.
2. **Plateaus & Shallow Minima**: Momentum lets the optimizer "coast" through flat regions where gradients are tiny, using kinetic energy stored from previous steeper regions.

---

## 1.3 Key Parameters and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value | Effect if Increased |
|:-------|:--------|:--------------|:--------------------|
| $\eta$ | Learning rate | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ | Faster but unstable |
| $\beta$ | Momentum coefficient | **0.9** | Smoother trajectory, larger effective horizon |
| $\gamma$ | Nesterov coefficient (NAG) | **0.9** | Look-ahead correction |
| $\mu$ | Damping (PyTorch) | $\mu = 1-\beta$ | Numerical stability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test the **default momentum value of 0.9** (proposed by Sutskever et al., 2013, used in AlexNet) — commit this to memory.

---

## 1.4 Why Not Just Use a Larger Learning Rate?

A common student misconception is that increasing $\eta$ alone solves slow convergence. In reality, $\eta$ controls **instantaneous step size**, while $\beta$ controls **memory of past directions**. Increasing $\eta$ amplifies *all* dimensions (including noisy ones), risking divergence. Momentum selectively amplifies *consistent* directions via exponential averaging.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

The momentum update unfolds in the following rigorous sequence at each iteration $t$:

1. **Sample / Compute the Gradient** at the current parameter vector $\theta_{t}$:
   $g_{t} = \nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})$
   *Why?* We need the local slope of the loss surface to know which direction reduces loss.

2. **Update the Velocity Vector** by combining the *previous velocity* with the *current gradient*:
   $v_{t+1} = \beta \, v_{t} + \eta \, g_{t}$
   *Why?* This is an exponential moving average (EMA). The past contributes geometrically: $\beta^{k}$ weight for the gradient from $k$ steps ago.

3. **Update the Parameters** by subtracting the velocity (not the raw gradient):
   $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - v_{t+1}$
   *Why?* Subtracting $v_{t+1}$ means we move along the *smoothed* direction, dampening high-frequency oscillations.

4. **Repeat** until convergence criteria are met (gradient norm below threshold, or max iterations reached).

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Unrolling — The "Effective Update"

Substituting $v_{t+1}$ repeatedly reveals that momentum is mathematically equivalent to a **weighted sum of past gradients**:

$$
v_{t+1} \;=\; \eta \sum_{k=0}^{t} \beta^{\,t-k} \, g_{k}
$$

This shows momentum essentially computes an **exponentially weighted moving average (EWMA)** of all historical gradients, then steps along that average.

> [!NOTE]
> **Effective horizon**: Since $\beta^{k}$ decays geometrically, gradients older than approximately $\frac{1}{1-\beta}$ steps contribute negligibly. For $\beta = 0.9$, the effective horizon is $\approx 10$ steps; for $\beta = 0.99$, it is $\approx 100$ steps.

---

## 2.3 Convergence Properties

For a **convex**, $L$-smooth objective with bounded gradients, GD with Polyak momentum achieves a convergence rate of:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\bar{\theta}_{T}) - \mathcal{L}(\theta^{\star}) \;\leq\; \mathcal{O}\!\left(\frac{\Vert \theta_{0} - \theta^{\star} \Vert^{2}}{T^{2}}\right)
$$

when the momentum parameter is chosen optimally. This is the **same rate as Nesterov's accelerated gradient (NAG)** but with simpler analysis — a frequent KTU question stem.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master every row below. KTU frequently combines them into a single numerical problem worth 7-14 marks.**

| # | Formula / Identity | Meaning / Use |
|:-:|:-------------------|:--------------|
| 1 | $v_{t+1} = \beta v_{t} + \eta \, \nabla\mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})$ | Velocity update |
| 2 | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - v_{t+1}$ | Parameter update |
| 3 | $v_{t+1} = \eta \sum_{k=0}^{t}\beta^{t-k}g_{k}$ | Unrolled (effective) update |
| 4 | $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\beta^{k} = \frac{1}{1-\beta}$ | Total weight (geometric series) |
| 5 | $\frac{\eta}{1-\beta}$ | Effective learning rate |
| 6 | $\tilde{v}_{t+1} = \beta v_{t} + \eta \, \nabla\mathcal{L}(\theta_{t} - \beta v_{t})$ | **Nesterov** accelerated gradient |
| 7 | $1-\beta^{T+1}$ | Finite-horizon normalization (PyTorch) |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|:-------|:------------|
| Computer Vision | Training ResNet, EfficientNet, YOLO |
| NLP | Fine-tuning BERT, GPT transformers |
| Reinforcement Learning | Policy gradient baselines (A2C, PPO) |
| Generative Models | GAN and Diffusion model training |
| Time-Series | RNN/LSTM training on financial/medical sequences |

> [!NOTE]
> Industry deep learning frameworks (PyTorch's `torch.optim.SGD(momentum=0.9)`, TensorFlow's `tf.keras.optimizers.SGD(momentum=0.9)`) implement **exactly** this algorithm. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to recognize the formula and explain the role of $\beta$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Unrolling the Momentum Update

**Goal**: Express $v_{t+1}$ explicitly as a function of historical gradients $g_{0}, g_{1}, \dots, g_{t}$.

**Step 1 — Write the recursive definition.**

$$
v_{t+1} \;=\; \beta \, v_{t} \;+\; \eta \, g_{t}
$$

**Step 2 — Expand one step backward.**

$$
v_{t} \;=\; \beta \, v_{t-1} \;+\; \eta \, g_{t-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into Step 1.**

$$
v_{t+1} \;=\; \beta\big(\beta \, v_{t-1} + \eta \, g_{t-1}\big) + \eta \, g_{t}
$$

$$
v_{t+1} \;=\; \beta^{2} \, v_{t-1} \;+\; \eta \beta \, g_{t-1} \;+\; \eta \, g_{t}
$$

**Step 4 — Continue expansion to $t=0$ (using $v_{0} = \mathbf{0}$).**

$$
v_{t+1} \;=\; \beta^{t+1} \, v_{0} \;+\; \eta \sum_{k=0}^{t} \beta^{\,t-k} \, g_{k}
$$

$$
v_{t+1} \;=\; \eta \sum_{k=0}^{t} \beta^{\,t-k} \, g_{k}
$$

**Conclusion:** The current velocity is an **exponentially weighted sum of past gradients**, where the most recent gradient carries weight $\eta$, the one before carries $\eta\beta$, and so on. *This is the exponential moving average (EMA) interpretation.*

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Effective Learning Rate Under Constant Gradient

**Scenario**: Suppose the gradient is constant, $g_{k} = g$ for all $k$, and $v_{0} = 0$. Find the steady-state step size.

**Step 1 — Write the unrolled form.**

$$
v_{t+1} \;=\; \eta \, g \sum_{k=0}^{t} \beta^{\,t-k}
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the geometric series.**

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{t} \beta^{\,t-k} \;=\; \sum_{j=0}^{t} \beta^{\,j} \;=\; \frac{1-\beta^{t+1}}{1-\beta}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the limit as $t \to \infty$.**

$$
\lim_{t \to \infty} v_{t+1} \;=\; \frac{\eta \, g}{1-\beta}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**

> The asymptotic step per iteration is $\dfrac{\eta}{1-\beta}$ times the gradient, instead of just $\eta$. Hence:
> $$\boxed{\;\eta_{\text{effective}} \;=\; \frac{\eta}{1-\beta}\;}$$
> For $\beta = 0.9$, the effective learning rate is **10× the nominal value** — this is why momentum allows *smaller nominal $\eta$* without sacrificing speed.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Bias Correction for Initialization

**Problem**: When $v_{0} = 0$, the early-iteration velocity is biased toward zero (warm-up phase).

**Solution**: Apply the standard EMA bias-correction factor used in Adam-style optimizers.

$$
\hat{v}_{t+1} \;=\; \frac{v_{t+1}}{1 - \beta^{\,t+1}} \;=\; \frac{\eta}{1 - \beta^{\,t+1}} \sum_{k=0}^{t} \beta^{\,t-k} g_{k}
$$

As $t \to \infty$, $\beta^{t+1} \to 0$ and the correction vanishes — confirming that bias only matters in the first few iterations.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Momentum vs Vanilla GD vs Nesterov

| Property | Vanilla GD | GD with Momentum | Nesterov (NAG) |
|:---------|:-----------|:-----------------|:---------------|
| Uses past gradients | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (EMA) | ✅ Yes (EMA) |
| Look-ahead correction | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Update rule | $\theta - \eta g$ | $\theta - v$ | $\theta - (\beta v + \eta g_{\text{look}})$ |
| Effective step | $\eta$ | $\dfrac{\eta}{1-\beta}$ | $\dfrac{\eta}{1-\beta}$ |
| Convergence (convex) | $\mathcal{O}(1/T)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1/T^{2})$ | $\mathcal{O}(1/T^{2})$ |
| Hyperparameter $\beta$ | N/A | 0.9 | 0.9 |
| Best for | Toy problems | Deep nets (default) | Deep nets (slightly faster) |
| KTU weightage | Low | **Very High** | High |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Style Calculation Practice:** Given $\eta = 0.1$, $\beta = 0.8$, $v_{0}=0$, and gradients $g_{0}=2.0$, $g_{1}=-0.5$, $g_{2}=1.5$. Compute $v_{1}, v_{2}, v_{3}$.

**Iteration $t=0$:**
$$
v_{1} \;=\; \beta v_{0} + \eta g_{0} \;=\; 0.8(0) + 0.1(2.0) \;=\; 0.20
$$

**Iteration $t=1$:**
$$
v_{2} \;=\; \beta v_{1} + \eta g_{1} \;=\; 0.8(0.20) + 0.1(-0.5) \;=\; 0.16 - 0.05 \;=\; 0.11
$$

**Iteration $t=2$:**
$$
v_{3} \;=\; \beta v_{2} + \eta g_{2} \;=\; 0.8(0.11) + 0.1(1.5) \;=\; 0.088 + 0.150 \;=\; 0.238
$$

> **Effective learning rate check:** $\dfrac{\eta}{1-\beta} = \dfrac{0.1}{0.2} = 0.5$ (verified asymptotically).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flow — Momentum-Enhanced Gradient Descent

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A(["Start: Initialize parameters theta_0, velocity v_0 = 0, learning rate eta, momentum beta"]) --> B["Set iteration t = 0"]
    B --> C{"Convergence criterion met?"}
    C -- "Yes" --> Z(["Output: Optimal theta_star"])
    C -- "No" --> D["Compute gradient: g_t = grad L theta_t"]
    D --> E["Update velocity: v_t+1 = beta * v_t + eta * g_t"]
    E --> F["Update parameters: theta_t+1 = theta_t - v_t+1"]
    F --> G["Increment t: t = t + 1"]
    G --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The crucial loop is the velocity update, where past information (via $v_t$) is combined with fresh gradient information. This is what differentiates momentum from vanilla GD.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Stage"]
        P["Parameter Vector theta_t"]
        G["Gradient g_t"]
        VT["Previous Velocity v_t"]
    end
    subgraph COMBINER["Velocity Combiner"]
        M1["Multiply by beta"]
        M2["Multiply by eta"]
        ADD["Vector Summation"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Stage"]
        VTP["New Velocity v_t+1"]
        THETA["Updated theta_t+1"]
    end
    VT --> M1
    G --> M2
    M1 --> ADD
    M2 --> ADD
    ADD --> VTP
    VTP --> THETA
    P --> THETA
```

---

## 4.3 Loss-Landscape Trajectory Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VANILLA["Vanilla Gradient Descent"]
        V1["Start"] --> V2["Step Right (zig)"]
        V2 --> V3["Step Left (zag)"]
        V3 --> V4["Slow descent"]
    end
    subgraph MOMENTUM["GD with Momentum"]
        M1A["Start"] --> M2A["Small zig"]
        M2A --> M3A["Zag damped"]
        M3A --> M4A["Smooth descent"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Vanilla GD oscillates across the narrow valley walls (zig-zag). Momentum smooths these oscillations because sideways gradient components from different steps partially cancel out in the EMA, while the consistent descent direction accumulates.

---

## 4.4 Information-Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INIT["Initialize: theta_0, v_0 = 0, beta, eta"] --> LOOP{"Iteration t active?"}
    LOOP -- "Active" --> GRAD["Compute gradient g_t"]
    GRAD --> EMA["Exponentially Weighted Average: v_t+1 = beta v_t + eta g_t"]
    EMA --> DECISION{"Gradient consistent?"}
    DECISION -- "Yes" --> AMPLIFY["Velocity amplified"]
    DECISION -- "No" --> DAMP["Oscillation damped"]
    AMPLIFY --> PARAM["theta_t+1 = theta_t - v_t+1"]
    DAMP --> PARAM
    PARAM --> LOOP
    LOOP -- "Inactive" --> END(["Converged: Return theta_star"])
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🔹 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define Gradient Descent with Momentum. State the role of the momentum coefficient $\beta$ in the update rule.**

**Model Answer:**
> Gradient Descent with Momentum is an optimization technique that augments standard gradient descent by maintaining a **velocity vector** that accumulates an **exponentially decaying moving average** of past gradients. The update rules are:
> $$v_{t+1} = \beta v_{t} + \eta \, \nabla\mathcal{L}(\theta_{t}), \quad \theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - v_{t+1}$$
> The momentum coefficient $\beta \in [0,1)$ controls how much of the past velocity is retained. A higher $\beta$ gives longer memory (smoother trajectory, larger effective horizon), while $\beta = 0$ reduces the method to vanilla GD. Typical value: **0.9**.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for equations, 1 for role of β)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Explain the physical analogy used to motivate momentum-based optimization. Why is "memory" useful in non-convex loss landscapes?**

**Model Answer:**
> The standard analogy is a **heavy ball rolling down a hill under fog**. Vanilla GD is like a person taking tiny, local slope-following steps, easily deflected by small bumps. Momentum gives the ball mass; it accumulates velocity in consistent directions and is *less* deflected by noisy gradients.
> **Why memory helps in non-convex landscapes:**
> 1. **Ravines:** Oscillations across narrow valleys cancel out in the moving average.
> 2. **Saddle points / plateaus:** Stored kinetic energy lets the optimizer coast past near-flat regions where instantaneous gradients vanish.
> 3. **Shallow local minima:** Sufficient momentum can roll *through* a shallow basin to reach deeper minima.

*(3 marks: 1 for analogy, 1 for non-convex issues identified, 1 for technical reasoning)*

---

## 🔹 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each Part B question below is divided into two sub-parts **(a) [7 marks]** and **(b) [7 marks]**, following the standard KTU ESE 2024 pattern. **You must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

---

### ⭐ **Question A** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** Derive the unrolled form of the momentum velocity update showing that $v_{t+1}$ is an exponentially weighted sum of past gradients. Comment on the role of $\beta^{t-k}$ weights. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Given $\eta = 0.05$, $\beta = 0.9$, and gradient sequence $g_{0}=4$, $g_{1}=3$, $g_{2}=2$, $g_{3}=1$ with $v_{0}=0$. Compute velocities $v_{1}, v_{2}, v_{3}, v_{4}$ and the corresponding parameter updates $\theta_{1}, \theta_{2}, \theta_{3}, \theta_{4}$ starting from $\theta_{0}=10$. **[7 marks]**

#### 📝 Model Solution — (a)

We begin with the recursive velocity update:
$$
v_{t+1} \;=\; \beta v_{t} + \eta \, g_{t}
$$

**Step 1:** Expand $v_{t} = \beta v_{t-1} + \eta g_{t-1}$ and substitute:
$$
v_{t+1} \;=\; \beta(\beta v_{t-1} + \eta g_{t-1}) + \eta g_{t} \;=\; \beta^{2}v_{t-1} + \eta\beta g_{t-1} + \eta g_{t}
$$

**Step 2:** Recursively substitute down to $v_{0} = 0$:
$$
v_{t+1} \;=\; \beta^{t+1}v_{0} + \eta\sum_{k=0}^{t}\beta^{t-k}g_{k}
$$

**Step 3:** Since $v_{0}=0$:
$$
v_{t+1} \;=\; \eta\sum_{k=0}^{t}\beta^{t-k}g_{k}
$$

**Comment on weights:** The weight $\beta^{t-k}$ applied to gradient $g_{k}$ shows that:
- The **most recent gradient** ($k=t$) carries full weight $\eta$.
- The gradient **one step ago** ($k=t-1$) carries weight $\eta\beta$.
- Gradients **$j$ steps ago** carry weight $\eta\beta^{j}$.
- This is precisely an **exponentially weighted moving average (EWMA)** with decay factor $\beta$, giving recent gradients higher influence while still retaining historical context.

#### 📝 Model Solution — (b)

Using $\eta = 0.05$, $\beta = 0.9$, $v_{0}=0$, $\theta_{0}=10$:

**Iteration 1:**
$$
v_{1} = \beta v_{0} + \eta g_{0} = 0.9(0) + 0.05(4) = 0.20
$$
$$
\theta_{1} = \theta_{0} - v_{1} = 10 - 0.20 = 9.80
$$

**Iteration 2:**
$$
v_{2} = \beta v_{1} + \eta g_{1} = 0.9(0.20) + 0.05(3) = 0.18 + 0.15 = 0.33
$$
$$
\theta_{2} = \theta_{1} - v_{2} = 9.80 - 0.33 = 9.47
$$

**Iteration 3:**
$$
v_{3} = \beta v_{2} + \eta g_{2} = 0.9(0.33) + 0.05(2) = 0.297 + 0.10 = 0.397
$$
$$
\theta_{3} = \theta_{2} - v_{3} = 9.47 - 0.397 = 9.073
$$

**Iteration 4:**
$$
v_{4} = \beta v_{3} + \eta g_{3} = 0.9(0.397) + 0.05(1) = 0.3573 + 0.05 = 0.4073
$$
$$
\theta_{4} = \theta_{3} - v_{4} = 9.073 - 0.4073 = 8.6657
$$

> **Effective learning rate** (theoretical steady-state): $\dfrac{\eta}{1-\beta} = \dfrac{0.05}{0.1} = 0.5$. As $t \to \infty$ with constant gradient, the velocity would approach $0.5 \cdot g$.

---

### ⭐ **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast Gradient Descent with Momentum and the Nesterov Accelerated Gradient (NAG). Write the update equations for both. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Discuss in detail how momentum helps in **three specific challenges** of deep network optimization: (i) ill-conditioned loss surfaces, (ii) saddle points, and (iii) noisy mini-batch gradients. **[7 marks]**

#### 📝 Model Solution — (a)

| Aspect | GD with Momentum | Nesterov Accelerated Gradient |
|:-------|:-----------------|:-----------------------------|
| Velocity update | $v_{t+1} = \beta v_{t} + \eta \, \nabla\mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})$ | $v_{t+1} = \beta v_{t} + \eta \, \nabla\mathcal{L}(\theta_{t} - \beta v_{t})$ |
| Parameter update | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - v_{t+1}$ | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - v_{t+1}$ |
| Gradient evaluation point | Current $\theta_{t}$ | Look-ahead $\theta_{t} - \beta v_{t}$ |
| Look-ahead | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Intuition | Heavy ball rolling | Smart ball that peeks ahead before stepping |
| Convergence (convex) | $\mathcal{O}(1/T^{2})$ | $\mathcal{O}(1/T^{2})$ (slightly faster empirically) |

**Key insight:** In standard momentum, the gradient is computed at the *current* position; in Nesterov, it is computed at the *anticipated next position*. This look-ahead acts as a correction term that reduces overshooting near the optimum, leading to faster practical convergence on deep nets.

#### 📝 Model Solution — (b)

**(i) Ill-conditioned loss surfaces (ravines):**
> Real loss surfaces in deep networks are highly anisotropic — some directions have steep curvature, others are flat. Vanilla GD oscillates severely along the steep directions. Momentum's EMA cancels opposing gradient components from consecutive steps along the steep direction while reinforcing the consistent descent direction. The effective learning rate $\eta/(1-\beta)$ accelerates progress along the shallow direction. *Practical example:* ResNet-50 training on ImageNet.

**(ii) Saddle points:**
> In high-dimensional non-convex optimization, saddle points are exponentially more numerous than local minima. At a saddle, $\nabla\mathcal{L} \approx 0$, so vanilla GD stalls. Momentum provides **kinetic energy** stored from previous iterations, allowing the optimizer to "roll through" the saddle region rather than waiting for the gradient to become informative again. Mathematically, the velocity term $\beta v_{t}$ is non-zero even when $g_{t} \approx 0$, ensuring continued descent.

**(iii) Noisy mini-batch gradients:**
> When using stochastic/mini-batch GD, $g_{t}$ is a noisy estimate of the true gradient $\nabla\mathcal{L}$. The noise has zero mean across many iterations. By taking an EWMA, momentum averages out this noise: random noise components partially cancel, while the true signal component reinforces itself. This effectively reduces the *variance* of the update direction, leading to smoother and more reliable convergence.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students LOSE marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the velocity initialization** $v_{0} = 0$ — Examiners deduct 1 mark if not stated.
> 2. **Mixing up the order** — Some students write $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \eta g_{t}$ and claim this is momentum. This is vanilla GD. The velocity update *must* precede the parameter update.
> 3. **Not stating the typical $\beta = 0.9$** — A free 1-mark booster if you mention it.
> 4. **Confusing momentum with Nesterov** — The look-ahead term $\nabla\mathcal{L}(\theta_{t} - \beta v_{t})$ is the giveaway for Nesterov. Don't swap them.
> 5. **Skipping the derivation step** — In 14-mark questions, the unrolling derivation must be shown step-by-step. Skipping directly to the final sum costs 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Incorrect unit consistency** in numerical problems — Round to 3–4 decimal places; do not truncate too early.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ⭐ **Two update equations** (memorize both):
  - $v_{t+1} = \beta v_{t} + \eta \, \nabla\mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})$
  - $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - v_{t+1}$

- ⭐ **Default momentum** $\beta = 0.9$, used universally in deep learning.

- ⭐ **Effective learning rate** = $\dfrac{\eta}{1-\beta}$.

- ⭐ **Unrolled form**: $v_{t+1} = \eta \sum_{k=0}^{t}\beta^{t-k}g_{k}$ (EMA interpretation).

- ⭐ **Three benefits**: (1) Ravine oscillation damping, (2) Saddle point traversal, (3) Noise reduction.

- ⭐ **Nesterov** modifies the gradient point: evaluate at $\theta_{t} - \beta v_{t}$.

- ⭐ **Initialization**: $v_{0} = \mathbf{0}$, $\theta_{0}$ = random or pre-trained weights.

- ⭐ **Geometric series identity**: $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\beta^{k} = \dfrac{1}{1-\beta}$.

- ⭐ **Convergence**: $\mathcal{O}(1/T^{2})$ for convex objectives with optimal $\beta$.

- ⭐ **Real-world deployment**: `torch.optim.SGD(momentum=0.9)`, `tf.keras.optimizers.SGD(momentum=0.9)`.

- ⭐ **Avoid confusion**: Momentum ≠ adaptive learning rate (that is Adam/RMSProp). Momentum only averages directions; it does not scale per-parameter.

- ⭐ **Physical analogy**: Heavy ball rolling down a foggy hill — remember this for 3-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nesterov Accelerated GD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT745 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Nesterov Accelerated GD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Nesterov Accelerated Gradient Descent (NAG)**, originally proposed by **Yurii Nesterov in 1983**, is a first-order optimization algorithm that improves upon classical *Momentum-based Gradient Descent* by introducing an **anticipatory (look-ahead) gradient correction** step. Instead of evaluating the gradient at the current parameter position, NAG evaluates the gradient at a *projected future position* along the direction of accumulated velocity, thereby providing a smarter, foresight-based update rule with provably better convergence rates ($O(1/k^2)$ versus $O(1/k)$ for smooth convex functions, where $k$ is the iteration count).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Nesterov Accelerated Gradient is grouped with **Adaptive Learning Rate / Optimization Techniques** in Module 2 of PEBMT745. Students must be able to derive the update rule, contrast it with classical momentum, and justify its accelerated convergence mathematically.

The governing update equations are:

$$
v_t = \gamma \cdot v_{t-1} + \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} J\bigl(\theta_{t-1} - \gamma \cdot v_{t-1}\bigr)
$$

$$
\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - v_t
$$

where:
- $\theta_t$ represents the **model parameters** at iteration $t$.
- $v_t$ is the **velocity vector** (accumulated gradient direction with momentum).
- $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ is the **momentum coefficient** (commonly set to **0.9**).
- $\eta$ is the **learning rate** (commonly set to **0.01** or **0.001**).
- $\nabla_{\theta} J(\cdot)$ is the gradient of the loss function $J(\theta)$ w.r.t. the parameters.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Cautious Hill Walker with Binoculars"**

Imagine two hikers trying to descend a foggy mountain (finding the minimum of the loss function):

1. **Classical Momentum Hiker (Naive):** He looks at the slope *directly beneath his feet*, then takes a big leap in the down-hill direction combined with his previous momentum. He tends to **overshoot** the valley and oscillate at the bottom.

2. **Nesterov Hiker (Smart, with Binoculars):** He first *projects* himself forward by one momentum-step (looks through binoculars to where he is *about* to be), checks the slope *there*, and only then takes his actual step. This **anticipatory correction** allows him to **slow down earlier** before overshooting the valley, reaching the minimum **faster and with less oscillation**.

The crucial insight: NAG does **not** blindly use the past velocity; it **corrects** the velocity using a gradient computed at a *forecasted* position. This is the heart of accelerated gradient methods.

## 1.3 Standard Hyperparameter Constants (Industry-Standard Defaults)

| Hyperparameter | Symbol | Default Value | Role |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Momentum Coefficient | $\gamma$ | **0.9** | Controls inertia / previous-velocity contribution |
| Learning Rate | $\eta$ | **0.01** (shallow nets) / **0.001** (deep nets) | Step size in parameter space |
| Velocity Initialization | $v_0$ | **0** | Initial velocity is set to the zero vector |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Nesterov? — The Motivational "Why"

Standard Momentum Gradient Descent computes the gradient at the **current** position and then moves along the accumulated direction. Mathematically, this can be reformulated as a **two-step** sequence:

**Step A (Naive):** Compute gradient at $\theta_{t-1}$ → update velocity → update parameters.

Nesterov's key observation: by the time the parameter $\theta$ is updated, the *velocity* has already shifted. So the gradient should be evaluated at the **look-ahead position** $\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}$, not at the *lagged* position $\theta_{t-1}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" in one line:** NAG = Momentum + Look-Ahead Gradient Evaluation. This single change yields an order-of-magnitude better convergence rate for smooth convex functions.

## 2.2 Algorithmic Logic — Step-by-Step Operational Flow

1. **Initialize** parameters $\theta_0$ and velocity $v_0 = \mathbf{0}$.
2. **For each iteration** $t = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, T$:
    - **Step 1 — Look-Ahead Projection:** Form the candidate position
      $$\tilde{\theta} = \theta_{t-1} - \gamma \cdot v_{t-1}$$
      This is the position we *would* be at if only the momentum term acted.
    - **Step 2 — Anticipatory Gradient Evaluation:** Compute the gradient of the loss at this *forecasted* position:
      $$g_t = \nabla_{\theta} J\bigl(\theta_{t-1} - \gamma \cdot v_{t-1}\bigr)$$
    - **Step 3 — Velocity Update (with corrected gradient):**
      $$v_t = \gamma \cdot v_{t-1} + \eta \cdot g_t$$
    - **Step 4 — Parameter Update:**
      $$\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - v_t$$
3. **Termination:** Stop when $\|\nabla_\theta J(\theta_t)\| < \epsilon$ or after $T$ iterations.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value / norm bars below are rendered using `\vert` and `\Vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table pipe syntax. KTU examiners specifically look for these formula formulations.

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Physical / Algorithmic Meaning** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Look-Ahead Position** | $\tilde{\theta} = \theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}$ | Forecasted parameter position using prior velocity |
| 2 | **NAG Velocity Update** | $v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla_{\theta} J(\tilde{\theta})$ | Combines inertia with look-ahead gradient |
| 3 | **NAG Parameter Update** | $\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - v_t$ | Final position move using corrected velocity |
| 4 | **Standard Momentum (for contrast)** | $v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_{t-1})$ | Gradient evaluated at *current* (not look-ahead) position |
| 5 | **Convergence Rate (Convex Smooth)** | $J(\theta_k) - J(\theta^{\ast}) \le \mathcal{O}\bigl(\tfrac{1}{k^{2}}\bigr)$ | Optimal first-order rate; $\theta^{\ast}$ is the optimum |
| 6 | **Convergence Rate (Momentum / Plain GD)** | $J(\theta_k) - J(\theta^{\ast}) \le \mathcal{O}\bigl(\tfrac{1}{k}\bigr)$ | Baseline rate for non-accelerated methods |
| 7 | **Effective Step Magnitude** | $\Vert v_t \Vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i} v_{t,i}^{2}}$ | Euclidean norm of accumulated velocity vector |
| 8 | **Gradient Norm Termination** | $\Vert \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_t) \Vert \le \epsilon$ | Stopping criterion (typical $\epsilon = 10^{-4}$ or $10^{-6}$) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Deep Convolutional Networks (CNNs):** Used in ResNet, DenseNet training pipelines to accelerate convergence and reduce training epochs.
- **Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) / LSTMs:** Helps mitigate vanishing/exploding gradient issues by smoothing updates.
- **Reinforcement Learning Policy Gradient Methods:** Nesterov momentum accelerates actor-critic convergence.
- **Large-Scale Generative Models (GANs):** Stabilizes adversarial training by dampening oscillations in the discriminator–generator game.
- **Computer Vision & NLP Production Systems:** Reduces wall-clock training time on large datasets (ImageNet, Common Crawl).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Standard Momentum to Nesterov Accelerated Gradient

We begin with the **standard momentum** update rule and systematically transform it to expose the look-ahead structure.

**Starting Point — Standard Momentum Gradient Descent:**

$$
v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_{t-1})
$$

$$
\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - v_t
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the parameter update into the velocity equation** to make the temporal dependence explicit:

$$
\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - \bigl[\gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_{t-1})\bigr]
$$

**Step 2 — Expand the right-hand side** to isolate the momentum and gradient components:

$$
\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1} - \eta \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_{t-1})
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the two competing contributions** in the update:
- The term $\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}$ is a *pure momentum-driven displacement* (no gradient).
- The term $-\eta \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_{t-1})$ is a *gradient-driven descent step*.

**Step 4 — Nesterov's Key Insight (1983):** Because the parameter $\theta_{t-1}$ on the right-hand side is *already one step behind*, evaluating $\nabla_{\theta} J$ at $\theta_{t-1}$ introduces a **lag error**. Nesterov proposed to evaluate the gradient not at the *current* position $\theta_{t-1}$, but at the **look-ahead position** where momentum alone would have carried us.

**Step 5 — Define the look-ahead position:**

$$
\tilde{\theta}_{t-1} \;=\; \theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\theta_{t-1}$ by $\tilde{\theta}_{t-1}$ in the gradient term:**

$$
\theta_t = \underbrace{\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}}_{\tilde{\theta}_{t-1}} \;-\; \eta \nabla_{\theta} J(\tilde{\theta}_{t-1})
$$

**Step 7 — Re-express as a clean two-line NAG update rule:**

$$
v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla_{\theta} J\bigl(\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}\bigr)
$$

$$
\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - v_t
$$

This completes the derivation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Convergence Rate Improvement — From $\mathcal{O}(1/k)$ to $\mathcal{O}(1/k^2)$

We establish the *optimal* convergence rate of NAG for smooth convex functions. The rigorous proof uses the **estimate sequence technique** (Nesterov, 1983); we present the key inequality and its implication.

**Step 1 — Definitions:**
Let $f(\theta)$ be convex and $L$-smooth (i.e., $\Vert \nabla f(\theta) - \nabla f(\phi) \Vert \le L \Vert \theta - \phi \Vert$). Let $\theta^{\ast}$ be a minimizer of $f$.

**Step 2 — Momentum-Sequence Construction:**
Define the sequence $\{\lambda_k\}$ of positive weights with $\lambda_0 = 0$ and recursion:

$$
\lambda_{k+1} = \frac{1 + \sqrt{1 + 4\lambda_k^{2}}}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Auxiliary (Estimate) Sequence Update Rule:**

$$
\phi_{k+1} = \theta_k + \frac{\lambda_k - 1}{\lambda_{k+1}}\bigl(\theta_k - \theta_{k-1}\bigr)
$$

**Step 4 — NAG Update (in the estimate-sequence form):**

$$
v_{k+1} = \theta_k - \frac{1}{L}\nabla f(\phi_{k+1})
$$

$$
\theta_{k+1} = v_{k+1} + \frac{\lambda_k}{\lambda_{k+1}}\bigl(v_{k+1} - v_k\bigr)
$$

**Step 5 — Lyapunov-Style Energy Inequality:** Nesterov proved that:

$$
f(\theta_k) - f(\theta^{\ast}) \;\le\; \frac{2L \Vert \theta_0 - \theta^{\ast} \Vert^{2}}{(k+1)^{2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Asymptotic Conclusion:**

$$
f(\theta_k) - f(\theta^{\ast}) \;=\; \mathcal{O}\!\left(\frac{1}{k^{2}}\right)
$$

versus the plain gradient-descent rate:

$$
f(\theta_k) - f(\theta^{\ast}) \;=\; \mathcal{O}\!\left(\frac{1}{k}\right)
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:** For a desired tolerance $\epsilon$, NAG requires only $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{\epsilon})$ iterations, whereas plain GD requires $\mathcal{O}(1/\epsilon)$ — a *quadratic* speed-up, explaining the name "accelerated" gradient.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Vector-Field Intuition (Geometric Derivation)

Consider the loss landscape $J(\theta)$ with curvature controlled by the Hessian $H = \nabla^{2}_{\theta}J$. Locally, the update direction is governed by the eigenvector decomposition of $H$.

**Step 1 — Local Quadratic Approximation:**

$$
J(\theta) \approx J(\theta_{t-1}) + \nabla J(\theta_{t-1})^{\top}\Delta\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\Delta\theta^{\top}H\,\Delta\theta
$$

**Step 2 — Gradient-Direction Decomposition:** The gradient at the look-ahead point $\tilde{\theta} = \theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}$ is:

$$
\nabla J(\tilde{\theta}) \approx \nabla J(\theta_{t-1}) - \gamma H\,v_{t-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Velocity Update Substitution:**

$$
v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta\bigl[\nabla J(\theta_{t-1}) - \gamma H\,v_{t-1}\bigr]
$$

**Step 4 — Algebraic Collection of $v_{t-1}$ Terms:**

$$
v_t = \eta \nabla J(\theta_{t-1}) + (1 - \eta \gamma)\, \gamma\, v_{t-1} + \mathcal{O}(\eta \gamma^{2} H v_{t-1})
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
- In directions of **low curvature** ($\sigma_{\min}(H) \to 0$), the term $H v_{t-1}$ vanishes and the NAG update reduces to standard momentum — building speed.
- In directions of **high curvature** ($\sigma_{\max}(H)$ large), the negative $\gamma H v_{t-1}$ term **damps** the velocity — preventing overshooting.

This explains *empirically* why NAG outperforms classical momentum on ill-conditioned (highly anisotropic) loss surfaces typical of deep neural networks.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: NAG vs. Classical Momentum

| **Property** | **Classical Momentum GD** | **Nesterov Accelerated GD (NAG)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Gradient Evaluation Point | $\theta_{t-1}$ (current) | $\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}$ (look-ahead) |
| Computational Cost per Step | One forward–backward pass | One forward–backward pass (identical) |
| Convergence Rate (Convex Smooth) | $\mathcal{O}(1/k)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1/k^{2})$ |
| Overshooting Tendency | High (oscillates near minima) | Low (anticipatory braking) |
| Sensitivity to Learning Rate $\eta$ | Moderate | Lower (more robust) |
| Implementation Complexity | Simple | Slightly higher (requires look-ahead compute) |
| Use in Deep Learning | Common, but rarely optimal | Preferred for production-grade training |

## 4.1 Update Trajectory Comparison (Mermaid Flow Diagram)

The following Mermaid block illustrates the **functional flow** distinguishing Classical Momentum from Nesterov Accelerated Gradient, side by side. The diagram is compiled using only safe alphanumeric node identifiers per the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards.

```mermaid

    nodeE[Nesterov: Compute grad at theta_tilde]
    nodeF[Update Velocity: v_t = gamma v_t-1 + eta times grad]
    nodeG[Update Parameters: theta_t = theta_t-1 - v_t]
    nodeH{Convergence Check: norm grad le epsilon}
    nodeI[Return Optimal theta]
    nodeJ[Continue Next Iteration]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB -->|Standard Momentum| nodeC

    nodeE --> nodeF

    nodeH -->|No| nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeB
    nodeH -->|Yes| nodeI
```

This block diagram represents the **vector-level correction** mechanism at the heart of NAG.


```mermaid

    axisA[Iteration k increasing]

    axisA --> curveC
    axisA --> curveD

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** The $\mathcal{O}(1/k^{2})$ decay of NAG versus $\mathcal{O}(1/k)$ of plain GD is not merely a constant-factor speed-up — for a target error of $10^{-6}$, NAG needs $\sim 10^{3}$ iterations while plain GD needs $\sim 10^{6}$ iterations, a **1000×** wall-clock improvement on smooth convex problems.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1, Module 2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between classical **Momentum-based Gradient Descent** and **Nesterov Accelerated Gradient Descent** in terms of where the gradient is evaluated.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
In classical Momentum GD, the gradient $\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_{t-1})$ is evaluated at the *current* parameter position $\theta_{t-1}$ and then combined with the previous velocity to update parameters. In Nesterov Accelerated Gradient (NAG), the gradient is evaluated at a **look-ahead (forecasted) position** $\tilde{\theta} = \theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}$, i.e., at the point where the parameters *would* be after applying only the momentum term. This anticipatory correction allows NAG to brake earlier near minima, yielding the optimal $\mathcal{O}(1/k^{2})$ convergence rate on smooth convex problems, compared to $\mathcal{O}(1/k)$ for classical momentum. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2, Module 2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q2.** State the **two update equations** of the Nesterov Accelerated Gradient optimizer and define each term.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$
v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla_{\theta} J\bigl(\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}\bigr) \quad \text{...(Velocity update)}
$$

$$
\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - v_t \quad \text{...(Parameter update)}
$$

- $\theta_t$: model parameters at iteration $t$.
- $v_t$: accumulated velocity (momentum) vector at iteration $t$.
- $\gamma \in [0,1)$: momentum coefficient (typically **0.9**).
- $\eta$: learning rate (typically **0.01** or **0.001**).
- $\nabla_{\theta} J(\cdot)$: gradient of loss $J$ evaluated at the *look-ahead* position. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style, 14 Marks, Module 2]**
> **Choose either (A) or (B).**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the Nesterov Accelerated Gradient update rule starting from the standard momentum update rule. Clearly explain each algebraic step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the convergence rates of Nesterov Accelerated GD, classical Momentum GD, and plain Gradient Descent for smooth convex functions. Provide the mathematical expressions and explain the geometric intuition behind the acceleration. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (A):**

**(a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Start with the standard momentum updates:

$$
v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_{t-1}) \quad\text{and}\quad \theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - v_t
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute the second equation into itself iteratively to expand:

$$
\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1} - \eta \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_{t-1})
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Identify the *momentum-displacement* term $\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}$ and the *gradient-correction* term $-\eta \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_{t-1})$.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Apply Nesterov's key modification: replace $\theta_{t-1}$ inside the gradient by the look-ahead position $\tilde{\theta}_{t-1} = \theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}$:

$$
\theta_t = \bigl(\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}\bigr) - \eta \nabla_{\theta} J\bigl(\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}\bigr)
$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Re-write in the canonical two-line NAG form:

$$
v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla_{\theta} J\bigl(\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}\bigr), \quad \theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - v_t
$$

**(b) — Convergence Comparison (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State the three rates:

$$
\text{Plain GD:} \quad f(\theta_k) - f(\theta^{\ast}) \le \mathcal{O}\!\left(\frac{L \Vert \theta_0 - \theta^{\ast} \Vert^{2}}{k}\right)
$$

$$
\text{Classical Momentum:} \quad f(\theta_k) - f(\theta^{\ast}) \le \mathcal{O}\!\left(\frac{1}{k}\right)
$$

$$
\text{Nesterov Accelerated GD:} \quad f(\theta_k) - f(\theta^{\ast}) \le \mathcal{O}\!\left(\frac{2L \Vert \theta_0 - \theta^{\ast} \Vert^{2}}{k^{2}}\right)
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Note the quadratic speed-up: to reach tolerance $\epsilon$, NAG needs $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{\epsilon})$ iterations, vs. $\mathcal{O}(1/\epsilon)$ for plain GD.

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Geometric intuition: classical momentum builds speed along low-curvature directions but overshoots near minima (gradient at lagged position). NAG evaluates the gradient at the *forecasted* position, allowing the optimizer to *anticipate* curvature changes and brake earlier, producing a smoother trajectory. On ill-conditioned landscapes, NAG's anticipatory correction counteracts the velocity build-up along high-curvature eigenvectors, eliminating oscillations.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain with a real-world analogy why Nesterov Accelerated Gradient Descent is termed an "accelerated" method. Justify your answer with reference to the role of the *look-ahead* position $\tilde{\theta} = \theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 2D quadratic loss $J(\theta) = a\theta_1^{2} + b\theta_2^{2}$ with $a = 10$, $b = 0.1$, learning rate $\eta = 0.05$, momentum $\gamma = 0.9$, and initial point $\theta_0 = (1, 1)^{\top}$, $v_0 = (0, 0)^{\top}$. Compute the first two NAG updates $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ step-by-step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (B):**

**(a) — Analogy (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State the analogy clearly: NAG is to classical momentum as a *cautious hill-walker with binoculars* is to a *naive hill-walker without binoculars*. Both want to descend a foggy mountain (minimize loss), but NAG *anticipates* the slope ahead.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Explain the *lag error* in classical momentum: since the gradient is computed at $\theta_{t-1}$ (one step behind), the optimizer is reactive rather than proactive. This causes overshoot in regions of high curvature.

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Justify the look-ahead position $\tilde{\theta} = \theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}$: this represents the *forecasted* trajectory of pure momentum. By evaluating the gradient at $\tilde{\theta}$, the optimizer gains *first-order information about the future*, applying a corrective braking force *before* overshoot occurs. This anticipation is the geometric origin of the $\mathcal{O}(1/k^{2})$ convergence rate, justifying the term "accelerated."

**(b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Setup (1 mark):** Loss $J(\theta) = 10\theta_1^{2} + 0.1\theta_2^{2}$. Gradient: $\nabla J = (20\theta_1, 0.2\theta_2)^{\top}$. Hyperparameters: $\eta = 0.05$, $\gamma = 0.9$, $\theta_0 = (1, 1)^{\top}$, $v_0 = (0, 0)^{\top}$.

**Iteration 1 (3 marks):**
- Look-ahead position: $\tilde{\theta}_0 = \theta_0 - \gamma v_0 = (1, 1)^{\top}$.
- Gradient at $\tilde{\theta}_0$: $\nabla J(\tilde{\theta}_0) = (20, 0.2)^{\top}$.
- Velocity update:

$$
v_1 = 0.9 \cdot (0,0)^{\top} + 0.05 \cdot (20, 0.2)^{\top} = (1.0, 0.01)^{\top}
$$

- Parameter update: $\theta_1 = \theta_0 - v_1 = (1, 1)^{\top} - (1.0, 0.01)^{\top} = (0, 0.99)^{\top}$.

**Iteration 2 (3 marks):**
- Look-ahead position: $\tilde{\theta}_1 = \theta_1 - \gamma v_1 = (0, 0.99)^{\top} - 0.9 \cdot (1.0, 0.01)^{\top} = (-0.9, 0.981)^{\top}$.
- Gradient at $\tilde{\theta}_1$: $\nabla J(\tilde{\theta}_1) = (20 \cdot (-0.9), 0.2 \cdot 0.981)^{\top} = (-18, 0.1962)^{\top}$.
- Velocity update:

$$
v_2 = 0.9 \cdot (1.0, 0.01)^{\top} + 0.05 \cdot (-18, 0.1962)^{\top} = (0.9 - 0.9, 0.009 + 0.00981)^{\top} = (0, 0.01881)^{\top}
$$

- Parameter update: $\theta_2 = \theta_1 - v_2 = (0, 0.99)^{\top} - (0, 0.01881)^{\top} = (0, 0.97119)^{\top}$.

**Final Answer:** $\theta_1 = (0, 0.99)^{\top}$ and $\theta_2 = (0, 0.97119)^{\top}$. Note how NAG rapidly damps the high-curvature $\theta_1$-direction ($a = 10$) and makes steady progress in the low-curvature $\theta_2$-direction ($b = 0.1$), demonstrating its adaptive damping property.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Look-Ahead Mistake:** Many students write $v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla J(\theta_{t-1})$ for NAG — this is the *classical momentum* rule, not NAG. The gradient **must** be evaluated at $\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}$. **Lose 2 marks** for this error.
>
> 2. **Convergence Rate Omission:** Students often quote $\mathcal{O}(1/k^{2})$ without specifying the *function class* (smooth convex) and *the constant* ($2L \Vert \theta_0 - \theta^{\ast} \Vert^{2}$). **Lose 1 mark** for vagueness.
>
> 3. **Momentum Coefficient Misuse:** Writing $\gamma = 0.99$ or $\gamma = 0.5$ without justification is penalized. State the **default value 0.9** and the allowed range $[0, 1)$.
>
> 4. **Numerical Computation — Sign Errors:** In Q(B)(b), students frequently forget that the look-ahead position is $\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}$ (subtraction, not addition). **Lose 1 mark** per occurrence.
>
> 5. **Confusing NAG with Adam/AdaGrad:** NAG is a *momentum method* that uses a fixed learning rate; Adam/AdaGrad are *adaptive* methods that scale the learning rate per parameter. KTU 2024 examiners may deduct marks if these are conflated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **NAG = Momentum + Look-Ahead Gradient Evaluation.** The gradient is computed at the *forecasted* position $\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}$, not the *current* position. *(High Frequency)*
- **Optimal Convergence Rate:** $\mathcal{O}(1/k^{2})$ for smooth convex functions — *the best possible* first-order rate (Nesterov, 1983). *(High Frequency)*
- **Two Update Equations to Memorize:**
  - $v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1})$
  - $\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - v_t$
- **Default Hyperparameters:** $\gamma = 0.9$, $\eta = 0.01$ (shallow) or $0.001$ (deep). *(High Frequency)*
- **Computational Cost:** Identical to classical momentum per iteration (still one forward–backward pass), so the convergence speed-up is *free*. *(High Frequency)*
- **Anticipatory Braking:** NAG corrects velocity *before* overshoot, unlike classical momentum which corrects *after*. *(High Frequency)*
- **NAG vs. Adam:** NAG is a *momentum-only* method with a global learning rate; Adam is *adaptive* with per-parameter learning rates and bias correction. They are not interchangeable.
- **Curvature-Damping Property:** NAG automatically damps velocity in high-curvature directions and accelerates in low-curvature directions — proven via the local quadratic analysis. *(High Frequency)*
- **KTU 2024 Module Coverage:** Belongs to *Optimization Techniques for Deep Learning* (Module 2). Frequently paired with **SGD, Momentum, AdaGrad, RMSProp, Adam** in exam questions.
- **No Learning-Rate Scheduling Required for Asymptotic Rate:** Unlike plain GD, NAG's $\mathcal{O}(1/k^{2})$ rate is achieved *without* learning-rate decay — a key theoretical advantage.
- **Origin:** Yurii Nesterov, 1983 (Soviet mathematical programming literature). Recast for deep learning by Sutskever, Martens, Dahl & Hinton (2013, ICML).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stochastic GD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT745 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stochastic GD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)** is a stochastic approximation of the standard gradient descent optimization method used to minimize an objective function (typically a loss/cost function) that is written as a sum of differentiable functions. Rather than computing the true gradient of the entire dataset (as in **Batch Gradient Descent**), SGD approximates the true gradient using **only a single randomly selected training sample** (or a small mini-batch) at each iteration.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, SGD is classified as a **first-order, iterative, stochastic optimization algorithm** that forms the foundational training engine for almost all deep learning models (CNNs, RNNs, Transformers, etc.).

Mathematically, the true cost function over a dataset of $N$ samples is:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L(\theta; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$$

In SGD, instead of summing over all $N$ samples, we pick one random index $i$ and update parameters using just that one sample's gradient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology for KTU Board Exams**
> - **Epoch** = One full pass through the entire training dataset.
> - **Iteration** = One single parameter update (occurs once per sample in pure SGD).
> - **Mini-batch** = A small subset of the dataset used per update (SGD = mini-batch size of 1).
> - **Learning rate ($\eta$)** = The step size taken in the direction of the negative gradient.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Blindfolded Hiker

Imagine you are a hiker stuck on a foggy mountain and you want to reach the lowest valley (the global minimum of the loss function).

- **Batch Gradient Descent** is like a hiker who *sees the entire mountain* before each step. They compute the steepest descent by checking the elevation at every single point on the mountain, then take one big, accurate, but extremely slow step.
- **Stochastic Gradient Descent** is like a hiker who is **blindfolded** and can only feel the slope of the ground **directly under their single foot** at any given moment. They take many small, fast, noisy steps. Some steps may go in the wrong direction (because one foot's slope is noisy), but overall they zigzag rapidly downhill, reaching the valley much faster.

The "noise" in SGD is actually a **feature, not a bug** — it helps the optimizer escape shallow local minima and saddle points in non-convex loss landscapes (like those in deep neural networks).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Stochastic"?** The word *stochastic* comes from the Greek *stochastikos* meaning "to guess" or "random." The randomness arises from the fact that the gradient is computed on a *randomly shuffled* sample, injecting noise into each update step.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used

- **Learning rate ($\eta$ or $\alpha$)**: Typically in the range $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-1}$. Standard starting value: **0.01** or **0.001**.
- **Momentum ($\beta$)**: Common default value **0.9**.
- **Mini-batch size ($m$)**: Powers of 2 are used in practice (e.g., **32, 64, 128, 256**) to align with GPU memory architectures.
- **Number of epochs**: Anywhere from **10 to 500** depending on dataset complexity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Mechanism

The SGD algorithm follows a precise sequence of operations for every iteration:

1. **Initialization**: Randomly initialize the weight vector $\theta$ (e.g., using He initialization, Xavier initialization, or simple Gaussian sampling). Optionally initialize velocity $v = 0$ if using momentum.
2. **Shuffle**: At the start of each epoch, randomly permute the order of the training dataset $\{(x^{(1)}, y^{(1)}), (x^{(2)}, y^{(2)}), \dots, (x^{(N)}, y^{(N)})\}$.
3. **Sample Selection**: For the current iteration $t$, pick a single training sample (or mini-batch) indexed by $i_t$.
4. **Forward Pass**: Compute the predicted output $\hat{y}^{(i_t)}$ using the current parameters $\theta$.
5. **Loss Computation**: Calculate the per-sample loss $L^{(i_t)} = L(\hat{y}^{(i_t)}, y^{(i_t)})$ (e.g., Mean Squared Error, Cross-Entropy).
6. **Gradient Computation**: Compute the gradient of the per-sample loss with respect to the parameters: $\nabla_\theta L^{(i_t)}$.
7. **Parameter Update**: Update parameters in the opposite direction of the gradient:
   $$\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \cdot \nabla_\theta L^{(i_t)}$$
8. **Convergence Check**: Evaluate whether the change in loss or the gradient norm falls below a predefined threshold $\epsilon$, or whether a maximum iteration/epoch count has been reached. If satisfied, stop; otherwise, return to step 3.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why" and "How" — The Core Intuition**
> - *Why shuffle?* To ensure that the order in which we sample does not bias the trajectory of descent. Without shuffling, the optimizer would cycle predictably and could fail to explore the loss surface properly.
> - *How does SGD escape local minima?* The stochastic noise in the gradient acts like a "random kick" that pushes the parameters out of shallow local minima where Batch GD would otherwise get permanently stuck.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Name** | **Formula / Definition** | **Typical Value / Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $J(\theta)$ | Cost function (full dataset) | $\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} L^{(i)}$ | Scalar (loss) |
| $L^{(i)}$ | Per-sample loss | $L(\theta; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ | Scalar (loss) |
| $\theta$ | Parameter vector | Updated each iteration | Real-valued vector |
| $\eta$ | Learning rate | Step size hyperparameter | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| $\nabla_\theta L^{(i)}$ | Stochastic gradient | Computed on single sample | Vector, same shape as $\theta$ |
| $\beta$ | Momentum coefficient | Decay factor for past gradients | 0.9 (common default) |
| $m$ | Mini-batch size | Samples per update | 32, 64, 128, 256 |
| $\gamma$ | Learning rate decay | $\eta_t = \eta_0 \cdot (1 + \gamma t)^{-1}$ | $10^{-3}$ typical |
| $g_t$ | Gradient at iteration $t$ | $\nabla_\theta L^{(i_t)}$ | Vector |
| $v_t$ | Momentum term | $\beta v_{t-1} + (1-\beta) g_t$ | Vector |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip**: When asked to "state the SGD update rule," always include the **learning rate $\eta$** explicitly. Examiners deduct marks if the update rule is written as $\theta = \theta - \nabla L$ without $\eta$.

## 2.3 Comparison: SGD vs Batch GD vs Mini-Batch GD

| **Property** | **Batch GD** | **Stochastic GD** | **Mini-Batch GD** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Samples per update | All $N$ | 1 | $m$ (e.g., 32) |
| Gradient noise | None (deterministic) | High | Moderate |
| Speed per update | Very slow | Very fast | Fast |
| Memory required | High | Low | Moderate |
| Convergence | Smooth | Noisy zigzag | Mild zigzag |
| Escapes local minima | Poorly | Best | Moderate |
| Used in practice today | Rarely | Sometimes | **Standard (e.g., Adam, RMSProp)** |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

SGD and its variants power virtually every modern deep learning application:
- **Computer Vision**: Training ResNet, VGG, YOLO object detectors.
- **Natural Language Processing**: Training RNNs, LSTMs, and Transformer-based language models.
- **Reinforcement Learning**: Policy gradient methods use stochastic updates.
- **Generative Models**: Training GANs and VAEs requires SGD-like updates due to the non-stationary loss landscape.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the SGD Update Rule

The SGD update rule emerges naturally from a **first-order Taylor expansion** of the loss function around the current parameter point.

### 3.1.1 Step 1: Linearize the Loss Function

Suppose we are at the current parameter value $\theta_t$ and wish to find a small perturbation $\Delta\theta$ that minimizes the loss. The first-order Taylor expansion around $\theta_t$ gives:

$$L(\theta_t + \Delta\theta) \approx L(\theta_t) + \nabla_\theta L(\theta_t)^{\top} \Delta\theta$$

### 3.1.2 Step 2: Minimize the Linearized Approximation

We want to find $\Delta\theta$ that minimizes $L(\theta_t + \Delta\theta)$. Since the linear approximation is unbounded below, we restrict the magnitude of $\Delta\theta$:

$$\min_{\Delta\theta} \quad L(\theta_t) + \nabla_\theta L(\theta_t)^{\top} \Delta\theta \quad \text{subject to} \quad \vert\vert \Delta\theta \vert\vert^2 \leq \epsilon^2$$

This is a standard constrained optimization. By the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, the minimum is achieved when:

$$\Delta\theta = -\eta \cdot \nabla_\theta L(\theta_t)$$

where $\eta$ is a small positive scalar (the learning rate) that scales the step to satisfy the constraint.

### 3.1.3 Step 3: The Final Update Rule

Therefore, the new parameter value is:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \Delta\theta = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_\theta L(\theta_t)$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4: From Batch to Stochastic

In Batch GD, the true gradient over the full dataset is:

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta_t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla_\theta L(\theta_t; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$$

This requires $N$ gradient evaluations per update — expensive when $N$ is millions.

In SGD, we approximate this sum with a single sample $i_t$ chosen uniformly at random:

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta_t) \approx \nabla_\theta L(\theta_t; x^{(i_t)}, y^{(i_t)})$$

The expected value of this stochastic gradient equals the true gradient:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[\nabla_\theta L(\theta_t; x^{(i_t)}, y^{(i_t)})\right] = \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t)$$

This is the key mathematical property that makes SGD a **valid** (though noisy) optimizer — it is an **unbiased estimator** of the true gradient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Unbiased Estimator Proof Sketch**:
> $$\mathbb{E}[g_t] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla_\theta L^{(i)} = \nabla_\theta J(\theta)$$
> The expectation of the per-sample gradient equals the population gradient because each sample is selected with uniform probability $\frac{1}{N}$.

## 3.2 Worked Example: SGD on a Simple 1D Loss

**Problem**: Minimize $L(\theta) = (\theta - 5)^2 + 2$ using SGD with learning rate $\eta = 0.1$, starting from $\theta_0 = 0$.

### Step 1: Compute the Analytical Gradient

$$\frac{dL}{d\theta} = 2(\theta - 5)$$

### Step 2: Iterative Updates

For a deterministic (Batch) setup with one sample, the update is:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot 2(\theta_t - 5) = \theta_t(1 - 0.2) + 1.0 = 0.8\theta_t + 1.0$$

### Step 3: Numerical Iteration

| **Iteration $t$** | **$\theta_t$** | **Gradient $2(\theta_t - 5)$** | **$\theta_{t+1}$** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.000 | $-10.000$ | 1.000 |
| 1 | 1.000 | $-8.000$ | 1.800 |
| 2 | 1.800 | $-6.400$ | 2.440 |
| 3 | 2.440 | $-5.120$ | 2.952 |
| 4 | 2.952 | $-4.096$ | 3.362 |
| 5 | 3.362 | $-3.277$ | 3.689 |
| 6 | 3.689 | $-2.621$ | 3.951 |
| 7 | 3.951 | $-2.097$ | 4.161 |
| 8 | 4.161 | $-1.678$ | 4.329 |
| 9 | 4.329 | $-1.342$ | 4.463 |
| 10 | 4.463 | $-1.074$ | 4.570 |

**Observation**: $\theta_t$ is converging toward the true minimum $\theta^* = 5$ (where the gradient is zero and the loss is $L = 2$). The geometric decay rate is $0.8$ per step, giving a smooth exponential convergence.

### Step 4: Convergence Verification

After 10 iterations: $\theta_{10} \approx 4.570$, with loss $L(4.570) = (4.570 - 5)^2 + 2 = 2.185$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a true **stochastic** setup, the loss curve would oscillate around the true minimum rather than smoothly approach it, because each per-sample gradient introduces random noise.

## 3.3 Convergence Analysis (Theoretical Bound)

Under standard assumptions (convex loss, decreasing learning rate $\eta_t$), the convergence rate of SGD is:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[J(\theta_T) - J(\theta^*)\right] = \mathcal{O}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{T}}\right)$$

where $T$ is the total number of iterations and $\theta^*$ is the optimal parameter set. This is **slower than Batch GD's $\mathcal{O}(1/T)$** rate for convex problems, but the **per-iteration cost is $N$ times cheaper**, making SGD far more efficient in practice for large datasets.

## 3.4 Variant: SGD with Momentum

To dampen the oscillations of vanilla SGD, we introduce a velocity term that accumulates past gradients:

$$v_{t+1} = \beta v_t + (1 - \beta) \nabla_\theta L^{(i_t)}$$

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot v_{t+1}$$

The momentum coefficient $\beta \in [0, 1)$ (typically $0.9$) smooths the trajectory by averaging out noisy gradients, analogous to a heavy ball rolling down a hill and gaining inertia.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define Stochastic Gradient Descent. How does it differ from Batch Gradient Descent in terms of computational efficiency?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is an iterative optimization algorithm used to minimize a loss function $J(\theta)$ by updating parameters using the gradient computed from a **single randomly selected training sample** at each iteration. The update rule is $\theta = \theta - \eta \cdot \nabla L(\theta; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$.
- **Computational Efficiency (1 mark):** Batch GD requires computing the gradient over all $N$ samples per update, costing $O(N)$ per iteration. SGD costs only $O(1)$ per update, making it vastly more efficient for large datasets, though it introduces noisy updates.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Explain the concept of "learning rate" in SGD. What happens if the learning rate is set too high or too low?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Concept (1 mark):** The learning rate $\eta$ is a hyperparameter that controls the step size of each parameter update. It is the scalar multiplier applied to the gradient before subtracting it from the current parameters.
- **Too high (1 mark):** If $\eta$ is too large, the optimizer may overshoot the minimum, causing divergence or oscillation around the loss landscape without convergence.
- **Too low (1 mark):** If $\eta$ is too small, convergence becomes extremely slow, requiring many more iterations to reach the minimum, and the optimizer may get trapped in poor local minima.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**Q (a)** Explain the Stochastic Gradient Descent algorithm in detail with its update rule. Discuss its advantages over Batch Gradient Descent. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Algorithm Description (3 marks):**

SGD is an iterative optimization method that minimizes a cost function $J(\theta)$ by updating parameters using gradients computed on one training example at a time. The full cost function over $N$ samples is decomposed as:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L(\theta; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$$

The algorithm proceeds as follows:
- **Step 1:** Randomly initialize parameters $\theta$.
- **Step 2:** Randomly shuffle the training data.
- **Step 3:** For each iteration, randomly select one training sample $i$.
- **Step 4:** Compute the per-sample gradient: $g_t = \nabla_\theta L(\theta; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$.
- **Step 5:** Update parameters: $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \cdot g_t$.
- **Step 6:** Repeat until convergence criterion is met.

**2. Update Rule (2 marks):**

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_\theta L(\theta_t; x^{(i_t)}, y^{(i_t)})$$

where $\eta$ is the learning rate and $i_t$ is a uniformly sampled index at iteration $t$.

**3. Advantages over Batch GD (2 marks):**

- **Computational Efficiency:** SGD performs $N$ updates per epoch versus Batch GD's single update, leading to faster convergence per epoch.
- **Memory Efficient:** Only one sample is loaded into memory at a time, making it suitable for large-scale datasets.
- **Escapes Local Minima:** The stochastic noise allows the optimizer to escape shallow local minima and saddle points in non-convex loss landscapes.
- **Online Learning:** Can be used when data arrives in streams, as updates occur per sample.

---

**Q (b)** Derive the SGD update rule from the first-order Taylor expansion of the loss function. Show that SGD is an unbiased estimator of the true gradient. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. First-Order Taylor Expansion (3 marks):**

Consider the loss function $L(\theta)$ at the current parameter $\theta_t$. For a small perturbation $\Delta\theta$:

$$L(\theta_t + \Delta\theta) \approx L(\theta_t) + \nabla_\theta L(\theta_t)^{\top} \Delta\theta$$

To find the optimal $\Delta\theta$ that minimizes this linearized loss, we apply the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality constraint $\vert\vert \Delta\theta \vert\vert \leq \eta$:

$$\Delta\theta^* = -\eta \cdot \nabla_\theta L(\theta_t)$$

Thus, the update rule becomes:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_\theta L(\theta_t)$$

**2. Unbiased Estimator Proof (4 marks):**

The true (batch) gradient is:

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla_\theta L(\theta; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$$

In SGD, the stochastic gradient is $g_t = \nabla_\theta L(\theta_t; x^{(i_t)}, y^{(i_t)})$ where $i_t$ is uniformly sampled from $\{1, 2, \dots, N\}$. The expected value of $g_t$ is:

$$\mathbb{E}[g_t] = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{N} \nabla_\theta L(\theta; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)}) = \nabla_\theta J(\theta)$$

Since the expected value of the stochastic gradient equals the true gradient, SGD is an **unbiased estimator** of the true gradient. This is the mathematical justification for why SGD converges to the true minimum despite its per-iteration noise.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` (Alternative Choice)

**Q (a)** Compare and contrast Batch Gradient Descent, Stochastic Gradient Descent, and Mini-Batch Gradient Descent with appropriate diagrams. Explain when each is most suitable. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| **Criterion** | **Batch GD** | **Stochastic GD** | **Mini-Batch GD** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Samples per update | All $N$ | 1 | $m$ (e.g., 32–256) |
| Update frequency per epoch | 1 | $N$ | $N/m$ |
| Gradient noise | None | High | Low–Moderate |
| Computational cost per update | High | Very low | Moderate |
| Memory requirement | Highest | Lowest | Moderate |
| Convergence smoothness | Smooth | Noisy | Mildly noisy |
| Escape from local minima | Poor | Best | Good |

**2. When to Use Each (2 marks):**

- **Batch GD:** Suitable for small datasets ($N < 10{,}000$) where the entire dataset fits in memory and exact gradient computation is required.
- **Stochastic GD:** Useful for streaming data, online learning scenarios, and when memory is severely constrained.
- **Mini-Batch GD:** The de-facto standard in modern deep learning — balances computational efficiency with stable convergence, and exploits GPU parallelism for batches of size 32, 64, or 128.

**3. Diagrams (1 mark):** Trajectory diagrams (refer to Section 4 of these notes) showing smooth path (Batch), zigzag path (SGD), and intermediate path (Mini-Batch) descending into the loss minimum.

---

**Q (b)** Explain the SGD with momentum algorithm. Derive its update equations and explain how momentum helps in faster convergence. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Motivation for Momentum (2 marks):**

Vanilla SGD suffers from high-variance updates, especially in ravines (regions where the surface curves more steeply in one dimension than another). It oscillates across the slopes of these ravines while making slow progress along the bottom toward the minimum. Momentum addresses this by accumulating a velocity vector in directions of persistent gradient reduction.

**2. Update Equations (3 marks):**

The SGD with momentum update equations are:

$$v_{t+1} = \beta v_t + \eta \cdot \nabla_\theta L(\theta_t; x^{(i_t)}, y^{(i_t)})$$

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - v_{t+1}$$

where:
- $v_t$ is the velocity vector (initialized to zero).
- $\beta \in [0, 1)$ is the momentum coefficient (typically 0.9).
- $\eta$ is the learning rate.

**3. Why Momentum Accelerates Convergence (2 marks):**

- **Smooths oscillations:** The velocity term averages out high-frequency oscillations in directions where the gradient sign frequently flips.
- **Accelerates consistent directions:** When the gradient consistently points in the same direction across iterations, the velocity builds up, allowing larger effective step sizes.
- **Physical analogy:** Like a heavy ball rolling down a hill — it accumulates kinetic energy and overcomes small bumps, while friction ($\beta$ factor) prevents infinite acceleration.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Missing the learning rate $\eta$** in the update rule: Writing $\theta = \theta - \nabla L$ instead of $\theta = \theta - \eta \cdot \nabla L$ → loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing SGD with Mini-Batch GD:** The KTU syllabus distinguishes SGD (sample size = 1) from Mini-Batch GD (sample size = $m$). Examiners test this distinction explicitly.
> 3. **Omitting the shuffling step:** SGD *requires* random sampling each iteration. If your algorithm description says "iterate sequentially from 1 to $N$," you have written Batch GD, not SGD → loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Failing to justify why noise helps:** When asked "why is SGD preferred," students often say "it's faster" without explaining the **local minima escape** argument. Always mention the regularization effect of stochastic noise.
> 5. **Forgetting the unbiased estimator property:** When asked for a derivation, you must show $\mathbb{E}[g_t] = \nabla J(\theta)$ — this is worth at least 2 marks in derivations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SGD** = iterative optimizer using gradient of **one random sample** per update.
- **Update rule:** $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \cdot \nabla_\theta L(\theta; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$.
- **Per-sample loss** $L(\theta; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ is used (not the full cost $J(\theta)$).
- **Learning rate $\eta$** is a critical hyperparameter: too high → divergence, too low → slow convergence.
- **Unbiased estimator:** $\mathbb{E}[\nabla_\theta L^{(i)}] = \nabla_\theta J(\theta)$ — the expected stochastic gradient equals the true gradient.
- **Convergence rate** for SGD on convex losses: $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{T})$.
- **Noise is a feature:** stochasticity helps escape local minima and saddle points.
- **Mini-batch GD** (most common in practice) uses $m$ samples per update where $1 < m < N$.
- **Momentum** ($\beta = 0.9$) smooths oscillations and accelerates convergence in ravine-like loss surfaces.
- **Data shuffling** at the start of each epoch is mandatory for SGD to behave stochastically.
- **One epoch of SGD** = $N$ parameter updates (versus 1 update in Batch GD).
- **Online learning** capability: SGD can update the model as new data arrives, unlike Batch GD.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
