<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Parameter Sharing** is a structural design principle in neural network architectures in which a single, identical set of learned weights (parameters) is reused across multiple computational positions, layers, or time steps of the model. The defining mathematical property is:

$$W_{i} = W_{j} \quad \forall \, (i, j) \in \mathcal{P}$$

where $\mathcal{P}$ denotes the set of positions that share the same parameter tensor $W$. The network does **not** maintain independent copies of $W$; only one tensor exists in memory and contributes to gradient updates from **every** location where it is applied.

**Parameter Tying** (also called *weight tying* or *weight coupling*) is a regularization constraint in which two (or more) **independent** parameter tensors are forced to remain equal or numerically close during training. The constraint is enforced either:

- **Hard tying** (exact equality): $W_{1} = W_{2}$ at every optimization step, or
- **Soft tying** (penalized closeness): the loss is augmented with a distance term $\lambda \, \Omega(W_{1}, W_{2})$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Both techniques address the same underlying goal — reducing effective model capacity and enforcing useful inductive biases — but they operate at **different layers of the model definition**. *Sharing* is an architectural property baked into the computational graph; *tying* is a training-time regularization constraint imposed on otherwise distinct parameters.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

**Parameter Sharing — The "Stamps" Analogy**
Imagine you are a postal clerk stamping "APPROVED" on 5,000 different documents. You do not create 5,000 individual rubber stamps carved with the word. You carve **one** stamp and press it onto every document. The single stamp is *shared*; the impression is reproduced everywhere identically. A Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) filter works the same way: one $3 \times 3$ kernel is slid across the entire image, stamping the same pattern-detection rule on every local region.

**Parameter Tying — The "Twin-Keyboards" Analogy**
Now imagine two keyboards belonging to two different people. They are physically separate devices (independent hardware), but the *firmware* installed on them is forced to be the same by a synchronization cable. The keyboards can drift apart for a fraction of a second, but the system continuously nudges their configurations back together. Parameter tying does the same: two weight matrices are independent tensors, but a penalty term in the loss function continuously pulls them toward each other.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction in One Line:** *Sharing* ⇒ one tensor used in many places (architectural). *Tying* ⇒ many tensors constrained to look alike (regularization).

### 1.3 Why These Techniques Matter in KTU Context

| Aspect | Without Sharing / Tying | With Sharing / Tying |
|---|---|---|
| Parameter count | Explodes with input size | Remains tractable |
| Memory footprint | High | Significantly reduced |
| Statistical efficiency | Low (more weights ⇒ more data needed) | High (fewer free parameters) |
| Translation / permutation robustness | Weak | Strong (especially for images and text) |
| Overfitting risk | High | Reduced |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education framework, this topic is mapped to **CO2: Design and train deep neural network architectures** and supports the cognitive level **Apply (L3)** in Bloom's Revised Taxonomy.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Parameters Are Shared

The motivation is rooted in two deep principles:

**1. The Equivariance Principle.** A learning task frequently exhibits a known symmetry. For example, in image classification, the label of a cat is invariant to the cat's spatial position — moving the cat three pixels to the right should not change the prediction. If the network contained a *unique* weight for every spatial location, the model would be forced to *re-learn* cat-detection independently at every position. By sharing a kernel across positions, the network is **equivariant to translation by construction**, so the cat is detected the same way regardless of where it appears.

**2. The Stationarity Assumption.** The underlying statistical property of the data is assumed to be locally stationary: the same local pattern (edge, texture, motif) can appear at any position in a sentence or any $(x, y)$ coordinate in an image. Sharing parameters encodes this prior directly into the architecture.

### 2.2 Where Parameter Sharing Is Used

| Architecture | Shared Entity | Position of Reuse |
|---|---|---|
| **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)** | Convolution kernel $K$ | Every spatial location of the feature map |
| **Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN/LSTM/GRU)** | Hidden-to-hidden matrix $W_{hh}$ | Every time step $t = 1, 2, \dots, T$ |
| **Transformers (Q, K, V projections)** | Per-head projection matrices | Every token position in the sequence |
| **Siamese Networks** | Feature-extractor backbone $\phi$ | Each branch of the twin network |
| **Graph Convolutional Networks** | Neighborhood aggregation weights | Every node in the graph |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are examinable in KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluations (ESE). Reproduce them without the vertical bar `\|`; use $\vert$ or $\mid$ when needed.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Domain | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Convolutional output size | $H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{H_{in} + 2p - k}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$ | Pixels (integer) | Compute feature-map dimensions in CNN design |
| 2 | Parameters in one conv layer (no sharing counted as one filter set) | $P_{conv} = k_{h} \cdot k_{w} \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out} + C_{out}$ | Scalar count | Compare against FC layer parameter count |
| 3 | Parameters in one FC layer | $P_{FC} = n_{in} \cdot n_{out} + n_{out}$ | Scalar count | Quantify the savings from sharing |
| 4 | Parameter-sharing ratio (CNN vs FC) | $R = \frac{P_{conv}}{P_{FC}} = \frac{k_{h} k_{w} C_{in}}{H_{in} W_{in} C_{in}} = \frac{k_{h} k_{w}}{H_{in} W_{in}}$ | Dimensionless ratio $\ll 1$ | Justify CNNs for image inputs |
| 5 | Hard parameter tying | $W_{dec} = W_{enc}^{\top}$ | Matrix equality | Autoencoder weight reuse |
| 6 | Soft parameter tying (penalty term) | $\Omega = \lambda \, \left\Vert W_{1} - W_{2} \right\Vert_{F}^{2}$ | $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ | Regularization in multi-branch nets |
| 7 | Frobenius norm used in tying | $\left\Vert W \right\Vert_{F}^{2} = \sum_{i,j} W_{ij}^{2}$ | Scalar | Penalty computation |
| 8 | Total parameters of tied autoencoder | $P_{AE}^{tied} = 2 \left( d_{in} d_{h} \right) + d_{h} + d_{in}$ | Scalar count | Compare to untied $4 d_{in} d_{h} + \dots$ |
| 9 | Effective DOF under tying | $DOF = \vert\theta\vert - C$ | Scalar | $C$ = number of equality constraints |
| 10 | RNN parameter count (vanilla) | $P_{RNN} = d_{x} d_{h} + d_{h}^{2} + 2 d_{h}$ | Scalar | Independent of sequence length $T$ |

### 2.4 Parameter Tying — Detailed Mechanism

**Hard Tying.** The architecture is defined such that the second weight tensor literally *is* a function of the first. In an autoencoder, a common choice is:

$$W_{dec} = W_{enc}^{\top}$$

This is implemented in code by *not allocating* a separate decoder weight matrix, but instead computing the decoder forward pass as $z W_{enc}^{\top}$. The autograd graph automatically back-propagates through this transposition, so a single update of $W_{enc}$ simultaneously affects both encoder and decoder behavior.

**Soft Tying (Penalty Method).** Two independently-initialized matrices $W_{1}$ and $W_{2}$ are kept distinct, but the optimization objective becomes:

$$\mathcal{L}_{total} = \mathcal{L}_{task} + \lambda \, \left\Vert W_{1} - W_{2} \right\Vert_{F}^{2}$$

The hyperparameter $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ controls the strength of coupling. As $\lambda \to \infty$, soft tying converges to hard tying. The gradient contribution of the penalty with respect to $W_{1}$ is:

$$\frac{\partial \Omega}{\partial W_{1}} = 2\lambda (W_{1} - W_{2})$$

which is added to the task-loss gradient during back-propagation.

### 2.5 Engineering Applications

- **Computer Vision:** Every state-of-the-art CNN (ResNet, EfficientNet, ConvNeXt) exploits parameter sharing via convolution.
- **Natural Language Processing:** ELMo and the original word2vec use tied input/output embeddings; modern LLMs often tie the embedding layer to the output projection head to reduce memory and improve sample efficiency.
- **Speech Recognition:** RNN-Transducer acoustic encoders share recurrent weights across time.
- **Biometric Verification:** Siamese networks share the verification backbone to project both inputs into a comparable embedding space.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently award marks for connecting theory to **real engineering systems**. Always cite *one* concrete application per answer.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Parameter Count in a Fully-Connected vs Convolutional Layer

**Setup.** Consider a single-channel grayscale image of size $H_{in} \times W_{in} = 32 \times 32$ fed into:

- **(a) A fully-connected layer** with $d_{out} = 1000$ output units.
- **(b) A convolutional layer** with $C_{out} = 6$ filters, each of kernel size $k_{h} = k_{w} = 5$, stride $s = 1$, no padding.

**Step 1 — Count parameters for the fully-connected case.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{FC} &= (H_{in} \cdot W_{in} \cdot C_{in}) \cdot d_{out} + d_{out} \\
&= (32 \cdot 32 \cdot 1) \cdot 1000 + 1000 \\
&= 1024 \cdot 1000 + 1000 \\
&= 1{,}024{,}000 + 1{,}000 \\
&= 1{,}025{,}000 \text{ parameters}
\end{aligned}$$

Each of the 1024 input pixels has an independent weight to every one of the 1000 output units.

**Step 2 — Count parameters for the convolutional case.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{conv} &= k_{h} \cdot k_{w} \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out} + C_{out} \\
&= 5 \cdot 5 \cdot 1 \cdot 6 + 6 \\
&= 25 \cdot 6 + 6 \\
&= 150 + 6 \\
&= 156 \text{ parameters}
\end{aligned}$$

The same 25 kernel weights (plus one bias per filter) are reused at every spatial position.

**Step 3 — Compute the reduction ratio.**

$$R = \frac{P_{conv}}{P_{FC}} = \frac{156}{1{,}025{,}000} \approx 1.522 \times 10^{-4}$$

This means the convolutional layer uses only **~0.015 %** of the parameters of the fully-connected equivalent — a $\sim 6{,}570\times$ reduction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The CNN has drastically fewer free parameters but achieves the same receptive-field coverage because each of the 6 filters is applied at $H_{out} \times W_{out} = 28 \times 28 = 784$ distinct spatial locations, with the **same 25 weights** in every location.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Back-Propagation Through a Shared Parameter

**Setup.** Consider a simplified network with a shared weight $w$ that contributes to two loss terms, $\mathcal{L}_{1}$ and $\mathcal{L}_{2}$, through two distinct computational paths. This is the exact situation that arises at every spatial location of a CNN filter.

**Step 1 — Write the total loss.**

$$\mathcal{L}_{total} = \mathcal{L}_{1}(w) + \mathcal{L}_{2}(w)$$

Because the same $w$ is used in both places, the gradient with respect to $w$ is the sum of contributions from both paths.

**Step 2 — Apply the chain rule.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{total}}{\partial w} &= \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{1}}{\partial w} + \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{2}}{\partial w} \\
&= \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{1}}{\partial z_{1}} \cdot \frac{\partial z_{1}}{\partial w} + \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{2}}{\partial z_{2}} \cdot \frac{\partial z_{2}}{\partial w}
\end{aligned}$$

where $z_{1}$ and $z_{2}$ are the pre-activation quantities at the two locations where $w$ participates.

**Step 3 — Concretize for a 1D convolution.** Let $w$ be a single kernel element used at positions $i$ and $i+1$:

$$z_{1} = w \cdot x_{i}, \qquad z_{2} = w \cdot x_{i+1}$$

Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial z_{1}}{\partial w} &= x_{i} \\
\frac{\partial z_{2}}{\partial w} &= x_{i+1} \\
\therefore \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{total}}{\partial w} &= \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{1}}{\partial z_{1}} \cdot x_{i} + \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{2}}{\partial z_{2}} \cdot x_{i+1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Generalize to a CNN filter used at $N$ positions.** The total gradient for a single filter element is the sum over all positions where it is applied:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{total}}{\partial w_{a,b}} = \sum_{i=1}^{H_{out}} \sum_{j=1}^{W_{out}} \delta^{(i,j)} \cdot x^{(i+a,\, j+b)}$$

where $\delta^{(i,j)}$ is the upstream gradient at output position $(i,j)$. This **accumulation of gradients from every spatial position** is the unique signature of parameter sharing in back-propagation.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Parameter Tying in an Autoencoder

**Setup.** An autoencoder has an encoder $E: \mathbb{R}^{d_{in}} \to \mathbb{R}^{d_{h}}$ and a decoder $D: \mathbb{R}^{d_{h}} \to \mathbb{R}^{d_{in}}$:

$$z = f(W_{enc} \, x + b_{enc}), \qquad \hat{x} = g(W_{dec} \, z + b_{dec})$$

**Step 1 — Untied parameter count.**

$$P_{untied} = (d_{in} \cdot d_{h} + d_{h}) + (d_{h} \cdot d_{in} + d_{in}) = 2 d_{in} d_{h} + d_{h} + d_{in}$$

**Step 2 — Apply hard tying constraint** $W_{dec} = W_{enc}^{\top}$. The decoder weight matrix is no longer a free variable; it is derived. The free parameters become:

$$P_{tied} = (d_{in} \cdot d_{h} + d_{h}) + d_{in} = d_{in} d_{h} + d_{h} + d_{in}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the savings ratio.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings} &= \frac{P_{untied} - P_{tied}}{P_{untied}} \\
&= \frac{d_{in} d_{h}}{2 d_{in} d_{h} + d_{h} + d_{in}}
\end{aligned}$$

For $d_{in} = 784$ and $d_{h} = 32$:

$$\text{Savings} = \frac{784 \cdot 32}{2 \cdot 784 \cdot 32 + 32 + 784} = \frac{25{,}088}{50{,}960} \approx 0.492$$

So tying saves about **49.2 %** of the autoencoder's parameters in this configuration.

**Step 4 — Gradient under hard tying.** Because the decoder weight is a function of the encoder weight, automatic differentiation computes:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{enc}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{enc}}\bigg|_{direct} + \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{dec}} \cdot \frac{\partial W_{dec}}{\partial W_{enc}}$$

For $W_{dec} = W_{enc}^{\top}$, $\frac{\partial W_{dec}}{\partial W_{enc}}$ is a transposition operator, and the encoder receives gradient signal from **both** the encoder path and the decoder path simultaneously.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table

| Scenario | Recommended Mechanism | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Image classification | Parameter **sharing** (CNN) | Translation equivariance + parameter efficiency |
| Sequence modelling | Parameter **sharing** (RNN cell) | Length generalization + memory bounded by hidden size |
| Encoder-decoder reconstruction | Parameter **tying** (autoencoder) | Forces symmetry; halves parameters |
| Multi-task learning with related heads | Parameter **tying** (soft) | Shares statistical strength without forcing exact equality |
| Biometric verification (two inputs) | Parameter **sharing** (Siamese) | Same feature extractor for both inputs |
| Large language model (embedding vs output) | Parameter **tying** | Reduces memory; often improves perplexity |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in KTU Answers:** Confusing "parameter sharing" with "parameter tying" or treating them as synonyms. They are **distinct** concepts. Sharing is **architectural**; tying is a **regularization constraint**.
