Shape HxWxC"] style inputA fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px style outputY fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#388E3C,stroke-width:2px style identityPath fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:3px style addNode fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#E64A19,stroke-width:2px style reluFinal fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#C2185B,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The yellow path is the **skip connection** (identity shortcut, no learnable parameters). It bypasses all three convolutions. The blue/red path is the **residual function** $F(x)$. Both merge at the orange "ADD" node. ### 4.2 Comparison: Plain Network vs. Residual Network — Backpropagation View ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PlainNetwork["PLAIN NETWORK - MULTIPLICATIVE GRADIENT FLOW"] L1P["L1"] --> L2P["L2"] --> L3P["L3"] --> L4P["L4"] --> L5P["L5"] --> L6P["L6"] --> LossP["LOSS"] LossP -.->|"tiny gradient
gamma to power L-1"| L1P end subgraph ResidualNetwork["RESIDUAL NETWORK - ADDITIVE GRADIENT FLOW"] L1R["L1"] --> L2R["L2"] --> L3R["L3"] --> L4R["L4"] --> L5R["L5"] --> L6R["L6"] --> LossR["LOSS"] L1R -.->|"skip identity"| L3R L3R -.->|"skip identity"| L5R L5R -.->|"skip identity"| LossR LossR -.->|"gradient preserved
via additive +1"| L1R end style L1P fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C style LossP fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C style L1R fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20 style LossR fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20 style ResidualNetwork fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:3px style PlainNetwork fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:3px ``` ### 4.3 Dense Connectivity Pattern in DenseNet ```mermaid flowchart TD INPUT["INPUT X0"] --> L1["DENSE LAYER 1
H1 convolution"] INPUT --> L2["DENSE LAYER 2
H2 convolution"] INPUT --> L3["DENSE LAYER 3
H3 convolution"] INPUT --> L4["DENSE LAYER 4
H4 convolution"] L1 --> L2 L1 --> L3 L1 --> L4 L2 --> L3 L2 --> L4 L3 --> L4 L4 --> OUTPUT["OUTPUT
Concat X0 X1 X2 X3 X4"] style INPUT fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1 style OUTPUT fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20 style L1 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100 style L2 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100 style L3 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100 style L4 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Connectivity Count:** In a 4-layer DenseNet block, the number of direct skip connections is $4 \times 5 / 2 = \mathbf{10}$, following the formula $\ell(\ell+1)/2$ for $\ell$ layers. Each layer receives feature maps from **all** preceding layers via channel-wise concatenation. ### 4.4 Highway Network Gating Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TD X["INPUT X"] --> TRANSFORM["TRANSFORM FUNCTION
H x with weights WH"] X --> GATE["TRANSFORM GATE
T x with weights WT
Sigmoid output 0 to 1"] TRANSFORM --> MUL1["MULTIPLY
H x times T x"] GATE --> MUL1 MUL1 --> ADDNODE{"ADD"} X --> ONEMINUS["ONE MINUS T x
Carry Gate = 1 - T x"] GATE --> ONEMINUS ONEMINUS --> MUL2["MULTIPLY
x times 1 - T x"] MUL2 --> ADDNODE ADDNODE --> Y["OUTPUT Y
Y = H T + x 1-T"] style X fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B style Y fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20 style GATE fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C style TRANSFORM fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17 style ADDNODE fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Gating Diagram:** When the transform gate $T(x) = 0$, output $Y = x$ (pure skip). When $T(x) = 1$, output $Y = H(x)$ (pure transform). The gate is *learnable*, so the network decides per-sample how much to transform vs. skip. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** **Define skip connection in a deep neural network. Why is it essential for training very deep networks?** **Model Answer (3 Marks — Board-Standard):** A **skip connection** (or shortcut connection) is a structural bypass in a neural network that allows the output of an earlier layer to be added to the output of a later non-adjacent layer, forming an alternative gradient pathway. Mathematically, for a residual block: $$ y = F(x, \{W_i\}) + x $$ It is essential for training very deep networks because of two key reasons: 1. **Mitigates Vanishing Gradients:** During backpropagation, the gradient through a residual block contains an additive "+1" term — $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial x = \partial \mathcal{L} / \partial y \cdot (1 + \partial F / \partial x)$ — which prevents the gradient from vanishing even when $\partial F / \partial x \to 0$. 2. **Enables Identity Learning:** If the optimal transformation for a layer is the identity function, the network can trivially achieve this by setting $F(x) = 0$, rather than learning identity through stacked nonlinearities — which is empirically difficult. *(Word limit advisory: 80–100 words for full 3 marks.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]** **Differentiate between identity shortcut and projection shortcut in ResNet. When is each used?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | **Aspect** | **Identity Shortcut** | **Projection Shortcut** | |---|---|---| | **Equation** | $y = F(x) + x$ | $y = F(x) + W_s x$ | | **Learnable parameters in skip path** | None | $W_s$ (typically $1\times1$ conv) | | **Used when** | Input and output dimensions are **identical** | Input and output dimensions **differ** (e.g., when stride = 2 for downsampling, or channel count changes) | | **Computational cost** | Zero overhead | Extra conv operation | | **Purpose** | Preserve gradient flow | Match dimensions AND preserve gradient flow | Projection shortcuts are deployed at the **start of each stage** in ResNet, where spatial dimensions are halved (via stride-2 conv) and channel counts are doubled (typically $C \to 2C$). --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- **Q3. (a) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Exam Style]** **With a neat architectural diagram, explain the working of a ResNet residual block. Derive the gradient flow equation and show how it mitigates the vanishing gradient problem. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Architectural Diagram (2 Marks):** ``` Input X ───────────────────────► (+) ──► ReLU ──► Output Y │ ▲ ├──► Conv3x3 ──► BN ──► ReLU ──┤ │ │ ├──► Conv3x3 ──► BN ───────────┘ └─────────────────────────────────── (Identity Skip) ``` *(The skip connection bypasses both conv layers; outputs are summed element-wise, then passed through a final ReLU.)* **Working Explanation (2 Marks):** 1. Input feature map $x$ of shape $(H, W, C)$ enters the block. 2. First $3 \times 3$ convolution with stride 1 and padding 1 produces $(H, W, C)$ feature maps, followed by Batch Normalization and ReLU. 3. Second $3 \times 3$ convolution (same parameters) extracts higher-level features, followed by Batch Normalization — *no* ReLU yet. 4. The original input $x$ is added element-wise to the BN output (this is the **skip connection**). 5. A final ReLU is applied to produce block output $y$. **Mathematical Formulation:** $$ y = F(x, \{W_1, W_2\}) + x = W_2 \cdot \sigma(W_1 x + b_1) + b_2 + x $$ **Gradient Derivation (3 Marks):** For backpropagation, applying the chain rule to compute $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial x$: $$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{\partial y}{\partial x} $$ Differentiating $y = F(x) + x$ with respect to $x$: $$ \frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial F(x)}{\partial x} + 1 $$ Therefore: $$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} \cdot \left( \frac{\partial F(x)}{\partial x} + 1 \right) $$ **Mitigation of Vanishing Gradients (3 Marks):** In a plain (non-residual) deep network of $L$ layers, the gradient flows multiplicatively: $$ \left\vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_1} \right\vert = \left\vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_L} \right\vert \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{L-1} \left\vert \frac{\partial F_i}{\partial x_i} \right\vert $$ If each $\vert \partial F_i / \partial x_i \vert < 1$ (common for ReLU/sigmoid activations), the product **decays exponentially** with depth. In a ResNet, the "+1" from the skip path **breaks this multiplicative collapse**. Even if $\partial F / \partial x \to 0$ (network has learned that the residual is small), the gradient still flows via the identity path with magnitude at least $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial y$. Thus: $$ \prod_{i=1}^{L-1} \left( 1 + \frac{\partial F_i}{\partial x_i} \right) \quad \text{is bounded away from zero} $$ This is the **mathematical proof** that ResNets of 50, 101, 152, and even 1001 layers are trainable, while plain networks degrade beyond ~22 layers. --- **Q3. (b) [Alternative Sub-Question]** **Compare the three major skip connection architectures: ResNet, Highway Network, and DenseNet. Discuss their mathematical formulations, gradient flow characteristics, and typical use cases. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | **Feature** | **ResNet** | **Highway Network** | **DenseNet** | |---|---|---|---| | **Year proposed** | 2015 (He et al.) | 2015 (Srivastava et al.) | 2017 (Huang et al.) | | **Skip mechanism** | Identity addition | Gated addition | Concatenation | | **Mathematical form** | $y = F(x) + x$ | $y = H(x) \cdot T(x) + x(1-T(x))$ | $x_\ell = H_\ell([x_0, \ldots, x_{\ell-1}])$ | | **Gating** | None | Sigmoid transform gate $T(x)$ | None (uses growth rate $k$) | | **Gradient flow** | Additive (+1 protection) | Gated multiplicative | Multiplicative via concat, but with **dense supervision** | | **Feature reuse** | Limited | Limited | **Maximum** (every layer sees all prior features) | | **Memory cost** | Moderate | Moderate | High (channel count grows as $k \cdot \ell$) | | **Typical use cases** | Image classification (ImageNet), detection backbones | Early LSTM alternatives, NLP | Medical imaging, small datasets, feature-rich tasks | **Detailed Comparison (Mark Distribution):** 1. **Residual formulation (2 Marks):** ResNet uses simple identity addition. The "+1" in the gradient is the key insight. It requires either dimension matching or a projection shortcut $W_s x$. 2. **Highway network gating (2 Marks):** Highway Networks introduce learnable gates that decide how much to transform vs. skip. The transform gate $T(x) = \sigma(W_T x + b_T)$ outputs values in $[0, 1]$. This is a precursor to LSTM-style gating. However, in practice, gating can collapse ($T \to 0$ for all inputs), negating the benefit. 3. **DenseNet feature reuse (2 Marks):** DenseNet takes skip connections to the extreme by *concatenating* (not adding) all prior feature maps. This maximizes feature reuse and reduces parameter count (narrow layers with growth rate $k = 12, 24, 32$). The trade-off is **memory cost** — channel count grows linearly with depth. 4. **Conclusion (1 Mark):** All three architectures leverage skip connections to combat vanishing gradients, but differ in their information integration strategy: ResNet (add), Highway (gate), DenseNet (concat). The choice depends on dataset size, memory budget, and feature complexity. --- ### 5.3 Module Internal Choice — Question A vs. Question B (14 Marks Each) --- **Question A [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]:** **(a)** Explain the concept of skip connection in deep neural networks. Discuss its role in the ResNet architecture with suitable block diagram. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the backpropagation gradient through a residual block and prove that skip connections prevent the vanishing gradient problem in deep networks. **(7 Marks)** *See model answer for Q3 above — this is the complete 14-mark treatment.* --- **Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style — Alternative Choice]:** **(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the architecture and working of **Highway Networks**. How do they differ from plain ResNet? Discuss the role of the transform gate and carry gate. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the **DenseNet** architecture in detail. Derive the formula for the total number of skip connections in a DenseNet block of $L$ layers. Discuss its advantages and limitations over ResNet. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer for Q.B(a):** **Highway Network Architecture (4 Marks):** A Highway Network, introduced by Srivastava et al. (2015), is a feed-forward neural network architecture that uses a **learned gating mechanism** to regulate the flow of information through the network. The output of a Highway layer is given by: $$ y = H(x, W_H) \cdot T(x, W_T) + x \cdot (1 - T(x, W_T)) $$ where: - $H(x, W_H)$ is the **transformation** applied to the input (analogous to a regular neural network layer). - $T(x, W_T)$ is the **transform gate**, a sigmoid-activated fully-connected layer: $T(x) = \sigma(W_T x + b_T)$, outputting values in $[0, 1]$. - $(1 - T(x))$ is the **carry gate**, which determines how much of the original input is passed through. **Role of Gates (2 Marks):** - When $T(x) \approx 0$ for some input: The layer outputs $y \approx x$ (acts as identity, no transformation). - When $T(x) \approx 1$: The layer outputs $y \approx H(x)$ (full transformation applied). - The gates are **input-dependent** and **learnable**, so the network can dynamically decide per-sample how much transformation is needed. **Difference from ResNet (1 Mark):** ResNet uses a *fixed* identity shortcut ($y = F(x) + x$, which is equivalent to $T(x) = 0.5$ always). Highway Networks make the shortcut strength *learnable* via the gate. This is more flexible but introduces extra parameters and the risk of gate collapse. **Model Answer for Q.B(b):** **DenseNet Architecture (3 Marks):** DenseNet (Densely Connected Convolutional Network), proposed by Huang et al. (2017), connects each layer to **every other layer** in a feed-forward fashion. For a block of $L$ layers, the $\ell$-th layer receives feature maps from all preceding layers: $$ x_\ell = H_\ell([x_0, x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{\ell-1}]) $$ where $[x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_{\ell-1}]$ denotes the **concatenation** of feature maps from layers $0$ to $\ell-1$, and $H_\ell(\cdot)$ is a composite function of BN-ReLU-Conv. **Skip Connection Count Derivation (2 Marks):** In a DenseNet block of $L$ layers, each layer $\ell$ is connected to all $\ell-1$ preceding layers. The total number of direct skip connections is: $$ \text{Total connections} = \sum_{\ell=1}^{L} (\ell - 1) = 0 + 1 + 2 + \ldots + (L-1) = \frac{L(L-1)}{2} $$ **Advantages (1 Mark):** - **Maximum feature reuse:** Every layer has access to all prior features → strong gradient flow. - **Parameter efficiency:** Narrow layers (growth rate $k = 12$–$32$) achieve competitive accuracy with fewer parameters than ResNet. - **Implicit deep supervision:** Each layer receives direct supervision from the loss via short paths. **Limitations (1 Mark):** - **Memory cost:** Channel count grows as $k \cdot L$, making DenseNet memory-intensive. - **Computational overhead:** Concatenation operations are not as optimized as additions in modern hardware. --- ### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Lose Marks on Skip Connection Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing skip connection with residual mapping:** A skip connection is the *pathway* itself; the residual mapping is the *learned function* $F(x)$. Examiners will deduct marks if these are used interchangeably. > > 2. **Forgetting the "+1" in the gradient derivation:** The term $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial x = \partial \mathcal{L} / \partial y \cdot (1 + \partial F / \partial x)$ is the **single most important equation**. Writing only $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial x = \partial \mathcal{L} / \partial y \cdot \partial F / \partial x$ will cost **2–3 marks** out of 7. > > 3. **Not specifying when to use projection vs. identity shortcut:** Examiners expect students to identify that projection shortcuts are needed when dimensions change (e.g., at stage boundaries in ResNet). > > 4. **Skipping the ReLU ordering detail:** ReLU should be applied *after* the addition $F(x) + x$, not before. This is a frequent 1-mark trap. > > 5. **Writing plain English for gradient equations:** Mathematical equations in proper LaTeX with explanation are mandatory. Vague "the gradient flows better" without derivation = **partial credit only**. > > 6. **Mixing up Highway, ResNet, and DenseNet equations:** Memorize the three distinct equations: > - ResNet: $y = F(x) + x$ > - Highway: $y = H(x) \cdot T(x) + x \cdot (1 - T(x))$ > - DenseNet: $x_\ell = H_\ell([x_0, \ldots, x_{\ell-1}])$ --- ### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Skip Connection Networks (Module 4)** - **Definition:** A skip connection is a feed-forward bypass that allows earlier-layer activations to reach deeper layers directly. - **Core Equation (ResNet):** $y = F(x, \{W_i\}) + x$ — always add input to the residual function output. - **Gradient Equation (Most Tested):** $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial x = \partial \mathcal{L} / \partial y \cdot (1 + \partial F / \partial x)$ — the "+1" prevents vanishing gradients. - **Three Major Variants:** - **ResNet** → identity addition (no learnable skip). - **Highway Network** → gated addition (learnable $T(x)$, $1-T(x)$). - **DenseNet** → concatenation of all prior feature maps. - **Skip Connection Count in DenseNet:** $L(L-1)/2$ connections for a block of $L$ layers. - **Identity vs. Projection Shortcut:** Identity when dimensions match; projection ($W_s x$) when dimensions differ (e.g., stride-2 downsample or channel doubling). - **Bottleneck Block Structure:** $1 \times 1 \to 3 \times 3 \to 1 \times 1$ with $4\times$ channel expansion at the end — enables ResNet-50/101/152. - **Key Benefit #1:** Mitigates vanishing gradients via additive gradient flow. - **Key Benefit #2:** Makes identity mapping trivially learnable (set $F(x) = 0$). - **Key Benefit #3:** Enables training of very deep networks (100+ layers) without degradation. - **ReLU Placement:** ReLU is applied *after* the addition, not before, to preserve the identity gradient pathway. - **Initialization:** He initialization ($\sigma = \sqrt{2/n}$) is standard for ReLU-based residual blocks. - **Architectures Built on Skip Connections:** ResNet, ResNeXt, DenseNet, U-Net, Highway Net, MobileNetV2 (inverted residual), EfficientNet (MBConv), Transformers (residual in every sub-layer). - **U-Net Skip Connections:** Skip connections in U-Net are between *encoder* and *decoder* at matching resolutions, concatenated (not added) — preserves fine-grained spatial information lost during downsampling. - **Exam Tip:** When asked "why skip connections?", always lead with the gradient equation and the "+1" term, then mention identity learning, then give real-world examples (ResNet-152 on ImageNet). - **Memory Aid:** *Skip = Shortcut = Stabilize = Scale* — these four S-words capture the role of skip connections in modern deep learning.

topics.title: Fully Connected CNN, etc.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT745 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Fully Connected CNN, etc. ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Fully Connected Convolutional Neural Network (FC-CNN)**, often simply referred to as a CNN classifier, is a hybrid deep learning architecture that combines the hierarchical feature extraction capability of **Convolutional Layers** (with pooling) and the high-level reasoning capability of **Fully Connected (Dense) Layers**. The convolutional blocks act as automatic, learnable feature extractors, while the fully connected layers act as the decision-making classifier that maps the learned high-level features to the final output class probabilities or regression values. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, the term *etc.* in *"Fully Connected CNN, etc."* encompasses: - The transition from Convolution/Pooling layers to the **Flatten layer** and **Dense layers**. - The mathematical formulation of forward propagation through Fully Connected layers. - The mathematics of **backpropagation** through these layers (gradient of loss w.r.t. weights, biases, and inputs). - Activation functions used at the dense stage (Softmax for multi-class, Sigmoid for binary). - Regularization techniques like **Dropout** and **Batch Normalization** applied to FC layers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A CNN without its Fully Connected head is just a *feature extractor*. A CNN with the FC head is a complete *end-to-end learnable classifier*. The FC layers learn the **non-linear decision boundaries** in the high-dimensional feature space produced by the convolutions. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Diagnosis System Imagine a hospital diagnostic process: 1. **Convolutional Layers = Specialist Doctors (Radiologists, Pathologists):** They look at the raw MRI scan/X-ray (input image) and extract structured medical features (tumors, lesions, bone density). They don't make the final diagnosis; they just describe what they see in a structured feature map. 2. **Flatten Layer = The Patient's Medical File:** The 3D feature map (Height × Width × Channels) is "unrolled" into a single long 1D vector, like compiling all the specialists' reports into one flat medical file. 3. **Fully Connected Layer = The General Physician:** The GP reads *every* report in the file and weighs them against prior medical experience (weights). Every neuron in the GP's brain is connected to every report in the file. 4. **Output Layer (Softmax) = The Final Diagnosis:** After reasoning, the GP outputs a probability distribution: "90% Pneumonia, 7% Bronchitis, 3% Normal." > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** Each FC neuron computes a **weighted sum of ALL flattened features**, applies a non-linear activation, and passes it forward. The "fully connected" property is what allows the network to learn **global dependencies** between features that the local convolutional kernels could not capture alone. ### 1.3 Critical Terminology | Term | Definition | | :--- | :--- | | **Flatten Layer** | Reshapes the 3D tensor $(H \times W \times C)$ into a 1D vector of size $H \cdot W \cdot C$. No learnable parameters. | | **Dense / FC Layer** | A layer where every neuron is connected to every neuron in the previous layer. | | **Weight Matrix ($W$)** | A 2D matrix of learnable parameters mapping input dimension $n$ to output dimension $m$. | | **Bias Vector ($b$)** | A 1D vector of learnable parameters (one per output neuron). | | **Softmax** | Activation that converts logits into a probability distribution over $K$ classes. | | **Dropout** | Regularization where random neurons are "switched off" during training to prevent overfitting. | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Architectural Flow of a Fully Connected CNN A standard CNN classifier (e.g., LeNet, AlexNet, VGG-style) follows this pipeline: $$ \text{Input Image} \rightarrow [\text{Conv} \rightarrow \text{ReLU} \rightarrow \text{Pool}]^{\times N} \rightarrow \text{Flatten} \rightarrow [\text{FC} \rightarrow \text{ReLU}]^{\times M} \rightarrow \text{Softmax} \rightarrow \text{Output} $$ **Step-by-Step Logic:** 1. **Feature Extraction Stage (Convolutional Base):** The input image $\mathbf{X}$ of shape $(H, W, C)$ passes through alternating **Conv-ReLU-Pool** blocks. Each block progressively reduces spatial dimensions $(H, W)$ while increasing the depth (number of channels). The output is a compact, semantically rich 3D feature map $\mathbf{F}$. 2. **Flatten Operation:** The 3D feature map $\mathbf{F}$ of shape $(H_f, W_f, C_f)$ is reshaped into a 1D column vector $\mathbf{x}$ of length $n = H_f \times W_f \times C_f$. This step preserves all information but loses the spatial structure. > [!NOTE] > **Why Flatten?** FC layers require 1D input vectors. The flatten operation is purely a reshape — no learnable parameters, no information loss. 3. **Fully Connected (Reasoning) Stage:** The vector $\mathbf{x}$ is fed into one or more Dense layers. Each Dense layer $l$ performs a linear transformation followed by a non-linear activation (typically **ReLU** for hidden layers). 4. **Output Layer:** The final FC layer has $K$ neurons (where $K$ = number of classes) and uses the **Softmax** activation to produce class probabilities $p_k$ that sum to 1. ### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' of Each Stage - **Why Pooling before FC?** Pooling reduces the feature map size dramatically. Without it, the FC layer would have millions of parameters, causing overfitting and computational explosion. For example, flattening the final VGG-16 feature map (7×7×512) gives only **25,088** inputs to the FC layer (manageable), but flattening a pre-pool map would give millions. - **Why ReLU in hidden FC layers?** ReLU ($f(z) = \max(0, z)$) mitigates the **vanishing gradient problem**, enables sparse activation, and is computationally cheap. Without non-linearity, stacking FC layers would collapse into a single linear transformation. - **Why Softmax at the output?** It maps arbitrary real-valued logits $\mathbf{z}$ to a valid probability simplex. This makes the output directly interpretable and compatible with the **categorical cross-entropy loss**. - **Why Dropout in FC layers?** FC layers contain ~80% of the parameters in a typical CNN and are the most prone to overfitting. Dropout (typically $p = 0.5$) forces the network to learn redundant, robust representations. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula | Notation | Purpose | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Dense Layer Forward Pass** | $\mathbf{z}^{[l]} = \mathbf{W}^{[l]} \mathbf{a}^{[l-1]} + \mathbf{b}^{[l]}$ | $W \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ | Linear transformation | | **ReLU Activation** | $\mathbf{a}^{[l]} = \max(0, \mathbf{z}^{[l]})$ | Element-wise | Non-linearity | | **Softmax Activation** | $p_k = \dfrac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$ | $K$ = classes | Probability distribution | | **Categorical Cross-Entropy Loss** | $L = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log(\hat{y}_k)$ | $y$ = true label | Loss for multi-class | | **Gradient w.r.t. Logits (Softmax + CE)** | $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}} = \hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}$ | Simplified form | Fast backprop | | **Gradient w.r.t. Weights** | $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{[l]}} = \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{[l]}} (\mathbf{a}^{[l-1]})^T$ | Matrix product | Weight update | | **Gradient w.r.t. Bias** | $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{[l]}} = \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{[l]}}$ | Sum over batch | Bias update | | **Gradient w.r.t. Previous Activations** | $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{[l-1]}} = (\mathbf{W}^{[l]})^T \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{[l]}}$ | Used for chain rule | Backprop signal | | **Weight Update Rule (SGD)** | $\mathbf{W}^{[l]} := \mathbf{W}^{[l]} - \alpha \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{[l]}}$ | $\alpha$ = learning rate | Gradient descent | | **He Initialization (for ReLU)** | $W \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{n_{\text{in}}}}\right)$ | $n_{\text{in}}$ = fan-in | Stable variance | | **Xavier Initialization (for Sigmoid/Tanh)** | $W \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n_{\text{in}}}}\right)$ | Variance balanced | For symmetric activations | | **Dropout (Inverted)** | $\mathbf{a}_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{\mathbf{r} \odot \mathbf{a}}{1-p}$ | $\mathbf{r} \sim \text{Bernoulli}(1-p)$ | Training-time regularization | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Note:** The combined gradient of **Softmax + Cross-Entropy** simplifies to $\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}$ (prediction minus truth). Examiners **love** asking this derivation. Memorize it. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Fully Connected CNNs are the workhorse of: - **Medical Imaging:** Tumor classification from MRI/CT scans. - **Autonomous Vehicles:** Traffic sign recognition (e.g., GTSRB dataset). - **Industrial Quality Control:** Defect detection on assembly lines. - **Satellite Imagery:** Land-use classification (e.g., EuroSAT). - **Document AI:** Handwritten digit recognition (MNIST), OCR systems. > [!NOTE] > **Modern Note:** In 2024-era architectures (ResNet, EfficientNet), the final FC layer is often replaced with **Global Average Pooling (GAP)** followed by a single linear layer to drastically reduce parameters. However, the classical FC head remains the KTU-recommended baseline and is essential for understanding. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Forward Pass Through a Single Fully Connected Layer Consider an input vector $\mathbf{a}^{[l-1]} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ (the output of the previous layer, which could be a flattened conv map or a previous dense layer's activations), a weight matrix $\mathbf{W}^{[l]} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$, and a bias vector $\mathbf{b}^{[l]} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$, where $m$ is the number of neurons in layer $l$. **Step 1: Linear Pre-activation** The pre-activation vector $\mathbf{z}^{[l]}$ is computed as a matrix multiplication followed by bias addition. $$ \mathbf{z}^{[l]} = \mathbf{W}^{[l]} \mathbf{a}^{[l-1]} + \mathbf{b}^{[l]} $$ **Explanation:** Each row $i$ of $\mathbf{W}^{[l]}$ corresponds to the weights of neuron $i$ in layer $l$. The dot product of this row with the input vector computes the weighted sum for that neuron. The bias $b_i$ shifts the result, allowing the neuron to fire even when all inputs are zero. **Step 2: Non-linear Activation** The activation vector $\mathbf{a}^{[l]}$ is obtained by applying an activation function $g(\cdot)$ element-wise to $\mathbf{z}^{[l]}$. $$ \mathbf{a}^{[l]} = g\!\left(\mathbf{z}^{[l]}\right) $$ For hidden layers, $g = \text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$. For the output layer with $K$ classes, $g = \text{Softmax}$ as defined in the formula sheet. ### 3.2 Derivation: Gradient of Categorical Cross-Entropy Loss w.r.t. Softmax Logits This is the **most important derivation** for KTU exams. We want to compute $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial z_k}$ where $L$ is the cross-entropy loss and $z_k$ is the $k$-th logit. **Step 1: Define the Loss** For a single training example with one-hot true label $\mathbf{y}$ and predicted probability $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \text{Softmax}(\mathbf{z})$: $$ L = -\sum_{j=1}^{K} y_j \log(\hat{y}_j) $$ **Step 2: Apply Chain Rule** We split the derivative using the chain rule over the softmax output $\hat{y}_j$: $$ \frac{\partial L}{\partial z_k} = -\sum_{j=1}^{K} y_j \frac{1}{\hat{y}_j} \frac{\partial \hat{y}_j}{\partial z_k} $$ **Step 3: Differentiate the Softmax** The softmax has two cases. For $j = k$: $$ \frac{\partial \hat{y}_k}{\partial z_k} = \hat{y}_k (1 - \hat{y}_k) $$ For $j \neq k$: $$ \frac{\partial \hat{y}_j}{\partial z_k} = -\hat{y}_j \hat{y}_k $$ **Step 4: Substitute and Simplify** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{\partial L}{\partial z_k} &= -y_k \frac{1}{\hat{y}_k} \cdot \hat{y}_k(1-\hat{y}_k) - \sum_{j \neq k} y_j \frac{1}{\hat{y}_j} \cdot (-\hat{y}_j \hat{y}_k) \\ &= -y_k(1-\hat{y}_k) + \sum_{j \neq k} y_j \hat{y}_k \\ &= -y_k + y_k \hat{y}_k + \hat{y}_k \sum_{j \neq k} y_j \\ &= -y_k + \hat{y}_k \left( y_k + \sum_{j \neq k} y_j \right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Use the One-Hot Property** Since $\mathbf{y}$ is one-hot, $\sum_{j=1}^{K} y_j = 1$, so $y_k + \sum_{j \neq k} y_j = 1$: $$ \frac{\partial L}{\partial z_k} = -y_k + \hat{y}_k (1) = \hat{y}_k - y_k $$ **Final Result (vector form):** $$ \boxed{\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}} = \hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is this beautiful?** This compact result eliminates the need to compute the full Jacobian of the softmax. It is the foundation of efficient backpropagation in classification networks. ### 3.3 Derivation: Backpropagation Through a Dense Layer Assume we have already computed the upstream gradient $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{[l]}} = \delta^{[l]} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ (the gradient of loss w.r.t. the pre-activation of layer $l$). We need three things: **3.3.1 Gradient w.r.t. Weights $\mathbf{W}^{[l]}$** $$ \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{[l]}} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{[l]}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{z}^{[l]}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{[l]}} $$ Since $\mathbf{z}^{[l]} = \mathbf{W}^{[l]} \mathbf{a}^{[l-1]} + \mathbf{b}^{[l]}$, the derivative $\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{z}^{[l]}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{[l]}} = (\mathbf{a}^{[l-1]})^T$. $$ \boxed{\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{[l]}} = \delta^{[l]} \, (\mathbf{a}^{[l-1]})^T} $$ For a batch of $B$ examples, this becomes $\dfrac{1}{B} \sum_{i=1}^{B} \delta^{i,[l]} (\mathbf{a}^{i,[l-1]})^T$. **3.3.2 Gradient w.r.t. Bias $\mathbf{b}^{[l]}$** Since the derivative of $\mathbf{z}$ w.r.t. $\mathbf{b}$ is just an identity, the gradient flows directly: $$ \boxed{\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{[l]}} = \delta^{[l]}} $$ For a batch, sum the gradients across the batch dimension. **3.3.3 Gradient w.r.t. Previous Layer's Activations (to propagate further back)** $$ \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{[l-1]}} = \frac{\partial \mathbf{z}^{[l]}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{[l-1]}} \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{[l]}} = (\mathbf{W}^{[l]})^T \delta^{[l]} $$ **3.3.4 Gradient Through the Activation Function** To pass the signal from $\mathbf{a}^{[l]}$ to $\mathbf{z}^{[l]}$ (or vice versa), we apply the derivative of the activation. For **ReLU**: $$ g'(z) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } z > 0 \\ 0 & \text{if } z \leq 0 \end{cases} $$ The local gradient is $\delta^{[l]} \odot g'(\mathbf{z}^{[l]})$ (element-wise). ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Complete Backprop Flow | Step | Computation | Shape (Single Example) | Purpose | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $\delta^{[L]} = \hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}$ | $(K, 1)$ | Output layer gradient | | 2 | $\delta^{[l]} = (\mathbf{W}^{[l+1]})^T \delta^{[l+1]} \odot g'(\mathbf{z}^{[l]})$ | $(n_l, 1)$ | Backprop through activation | | 3 | $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{[l]}} = \delta^{[l]} (\mathbf{a}^{[l-1]})^T$ | $(n_l, n_{l-1})$ | Compute weight gradient | | 4 | $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{[l]}} = \delta^{[l]}$ | $(n_l, 1)$ | Compute bias gradient | | 5 | $\mathbf{W}^{[l]} \leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{[l]} - \alpha \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{[l]}}$ | $(n_l, n_{l-1})$ | Update weights | | 6 | $\mathbf{b}^{[l]} \leftarrow \mathbf{b}^{[l]} - \alpha \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{[l]}}$ | $(n_l, 1)$ | Update biases | ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Conceptual) Consider a 3-class problem with logits $\mathbf{z} = [2.0, 1.0, 0.1]$ and true label $\mathbf{y} = [1, 0, 0]$. **Step 1: Compute Softmax** $$ \hat{y}_1 = \frac{e^2}{e^2 + e^1 + e^{0.1}} = \frac{7.389}{11.426} \approx 0.647 $$ Similarly, $\hat{y}_2 \approx 0.236$, $\hat{y}_3 \approx 0.097$. (Check: sums to 1.) **Step 2: Compute Cross-Entropy Loss** $$ L = -[1 \cdot \log(0.647) + 0 + 0] = -\log(0.647) \approx 0.436 $$ **Step 3: Compute Gradient** $$ \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}} = [0.647 - 1, \; 0.236 - 0, \; 0.097 - 0] = [-0.353, \; 0.236, \; 0.097] $$ This gradient now flows backward to update the weight matrix of the final FC layer. > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The negative gradient for class 1 (the true class) means weights should push $\hat{y}_1$ upward; the positive gradients for classes 2 and 3 mean weights should push those predictions downward. This is exactly what gradient descent does in the next step. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 End-to-End FC-CNN Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Image
224x224x3] --> B[Conv Block 1
Conv-ReLU-Pool
112x112x64] B --> C[Conv Block 2
Conv-ReLU-Pool
56x56x128] C --> D[Conv Block N
Conv-ReLU-Pool
7x7x512] D --> E[Flatten Layer
25088-dim vector] E --> F[FC Layer 1
4096 neurons + ReLU] F --> G[Dropout 0.5] G --> H[FC Layer 2
4096 neurons + ReLU] H --> I[Dropout 0.5] I --> J[Output FC Layer
K neurons + Softmax] J --> K[Class Probabilities
p1, p2, ..., pK] ``` ### 4.2 Inside a Single Fully Connected Layer (Neuron-Level View) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Input["Input Vector a_prev (size n)"] I1((x1)) I2((x2)) I3((x3)) I4((...)) I5((xn)) end subgraph LayerL["Dense Layer l (m neurons)"] N1((z1)) --> A1((a1)) N2((z2)) --> A2((a2)) Nm((zm)) --> Am((am)) end I1 --> N1 I1 --> N2 I1 --> Nm I2 --> N1 I2 --> N2 I2 --> Nm I3 --> N1 I3 --> N2 I3 --> Nm I4 --> N1 I4 --> N2 I4 --> Nm I5 --> N1 I5 --> N2 I5 --> Nm ``` ### 4.3 Backpropagation Signal Flow in FC Layers ```mermaid flowchart RL L[Loss L] --> ZL[Output Logits zL] ZL --> AL[Softmax Output yhat] AL --> L ZL -->|"delta_L = yhat - y"| WL[Weight Matrix WL] WL -->|"propagated gradient"| ZLm1[Previous Layer Logits zL-1] ZLm1 -->|"chain rule with ReLU derivative"| ALm1[Previous Activations aL-1] ALm1 -->|"reshape to feature map"| CONV[Convolutional Base for backprop] ``` ### 4.4 Parameter Count Comparison ```mermaid graph LR A[Conv Feature Map
7x7x512 = 25088] -->|Flatten| B[Vector 25088-dim] B -->|FC 4096| C[Hidden Layer
25088 x 4096 = 102.7M params] C -->|FC 4096| D[Hidden Layer
4096 x 4096 = 16.8M params] D -->|FC 1000| E[Output
4096 x 1000 = 4.1M params] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Total FC parameters: ~123.6 million** — this is why the FC head dominates CNN parameter counts and why techniques like GAP, Dropout, and weight decay are critical for FC-heavy architectures. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]** *What is the purpose of the Flatten layer in a CNN? What would happen if it were removed?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Flatten layer reshapes the 3D output of the final pooling layer (shape $H \times W \times C$) into a 1D vector of length $H \times W \times C$ so it can be fed into the Fully Connected (Dense) layers, which require 1D input. **(1.5 marks)** It contains no learnable parameters. If removed, the data would remain in 3D tensor form and the Dense layer (which expects 1D vectors and uses matrix multiplication) would raise a **shape mismatch error**. **(1.5 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** *Differentiate between the Softmax and Sigmoid activation functions. In a Fully Connected CNN, where is each one used?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Sigmoid** $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ maps a single real value to $(0, 1)$ independently. It is used in the **output layer for binary classification** (one neuron) and was historically used in hidden layers. **(1.5 marks)** **Softmax** $p_k = \dfrac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ maps a vector of $K$ logits to a **probability distribution** that sums to 1. It is used in the **output layer of multi-class classification** (one neuron per class) and is the standard pairing with categorical cross-entropy loss. **(1.5 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4 Pattern]** **(a)** Explain the architecture of a Fully Connected Convolutional Neural Network with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of each component. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the gradient of the Categorical Cross-Entropy loss with respect to the Softmax logits and show that it simplifies to $\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Architecture of Fully Connected CNN (7 marks):** A Fully Connected CNN is composed of two distinct stages: the **Convolutional Base** (feature extractor) and the **Fully Connected Head** (classifier). 1. **Input Layer:** Accepts an image tensor of shape $(H, W, C)$ — for example, $(224, 224, 3)$ for a standard RGB image. **(0.5 marks)** 2. **Convolutional Blocks:** A sequence of `Conv → ReLU → Pool` blocks. Each conv layer applies learnable filters to extract local patterns (edges, textures, parts). The ReLU introduces non-linearity, and the pooling layer (typically max-pooling with $2 \times 2$ stride) progressively reduces spatial dimensions, increasing the receptive field. The output of the final block is a compact 3D feature map, e.g., $(7, 7, 512)$. **(2 marks)** 3. **Flatten Layer:** Reshapes the 3D feature map of size $H_f \times W_f \times C_f$ into a 1D vector of length $n = H_f \cdot W_f \cdot C_f$ (e.g., 25,088 for a $7 \times 7 \times 512$ map). No parameters are learned here. **(1 mark)** 4. **Fully Connected Hidden Layers:** One or more Dense layers (e.g., 4096 neurons each in VGG). Each neuron computes a weighted sum of all inputs from the previous layer, adds a bias, and applies ReLU. **(1.5 marks)** 5. **Dropout Layers:** Inserted after FC layers with probability $p = 0.5$ during training to randomly zero out neurons, preventing co-adaptation and overfitting. **(1 mark)** 6. **Output Layer:** A final FC layer with $K$ neurons (where $K$ = number of classes) followed by a **Softmax** activation that produces class probabilities $\hat{y}_k$ summing to 1. **(1 mark)** *Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for a visual representation.* --- **(b) Derivation of Softmax + Cross-Entropy Gradient (7 marks):** *(This is the same derivation as in Section 3.2; reproduced here as the model answer.)* **Step 1 (0.5 marks):** State the loss and softmax definitions. $$ L = -\sum_{j=1}^{K} y_j \log(\hat{y}_j), \qquad \hat{y}_j = \frac{e^{z_j}}{\sum_{m=1}^{K} e^{z_m}} $$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply the chain rule to split the derivative through $\hat{y}_j$: $$ \frac{\partial L}{\partial z_k} = -\sum_{j=1}^{K} y_j \frac{1}{\hat{y}_j} \frac{\partial \hat{y}_j}{\partial z_k} $$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the partial derivatives of the softmax. For $j = k$: $$ \frac{\partial \hat{y}_k}{\partial z_k} = \hat{y}_k(1 - \hat{y}_k) $$ For $j \neq k$: $$ \frac{\partial \hat{y}_j}{\partial z_k} = -\hat{y}_j \hat{y}_k $$ **Step 4 (2 marks):** Substitute and split the sum: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{\partial L}{\partial z_k} &= -y_k \frac{1}{\hat{y}_k} \cdot \hat{y}_k(1 - \hat{y}_k) - \sum_{j \neq k} y_j \frac{1}{\hat{y}_j} \cdot (-\hat{y}_j \hat{y}_k) \\ &= -y_k(1 - \hat{y}_k) + \hat{y}_k \sum_{j \neq k} y_j \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Use the property that $\sum_{j=1}^{K} y_j = 1$ (one-hot encoding): $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{\partial L}{\partial z_k} &= -y_k + y_k \hat{y}_k + \hat{y}_k(1 - y_k) \\ &= -y_k + \hat{y}_k = \hat{y}_k - y_k \end{aligned} $$ **Final Result (0.5 marks):** $$ \boxed{\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}} = \hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}} $$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Pattern]** **(a)** Explain the forward propagation equations for a Fully Connected layer with $n$ inputs and $m$ outputs. Include the role of weight matrix, bias vector, and activation function. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With suitable equations, explain how backpropagation is performed through a Fully Connected layer. Show how the gradients of loss with respect to weights, biases, and previous layer activations are computed. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Forward Propagation through a Dense Layer (7 marks):** Given an input activation vector $\mathbf{a}^{[l-1]} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ from the previous layer (which could be a flatten layer or another dense layer): **Step 1 — Linear Pre-activation (2 marks):** $$ \mathbf{z}^{[l]} = \mathbf{W}^{[l]} \mathbf{a}^{[l-1]} + \mathbf{b}^{[l]} $$ where $\mathbf{W}^{[l]} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the weight matrix, $\mathbf{b}^{[l]} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the bias vector, and $\mathbf{z}^{[l]} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the pre-activation vector. The entry $W_{ij}^{[l]}$ represents the weight connecting input $j$ to neuron $i$ in layer $l$. **Step 2 — Non-linear Activation (2 marks):** $$ \mathbf{a}^{[l]} = g(\mathbf{z}^{[l]}) $$ For **hidden FC layers**, $g = \text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$, which introduces non-linearity and mitigates vanishing gradients. For the **output layer**, $g = \text{Softmax}(z_k) = \dfrac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ for multi-class problems. **Step 3 — Interpretation (1.5 marks):** Each output neuron $i$ computes a weighted sum of all $n$ inputs, adds a bias, and applies a non-linearity. This "fully connected" structure means every input feature influences every output neuron, enabling the layer to learn **global feature combinations**. **Step 4 — Example dimensions (1.5 marks):** For AlexNet's first FC layer: input dim $n = 9216$ (from a $6 \times 6 \times 256$ feature map), output dim $m = 4096$. The weight matrix has $9216 \times 4096 \approx 37.7$ million parameters — illustrating why FC layers dominate parameter counts. --- **(b) Backpropagation through a Dense Layer (7 marks):** Let $\delta^{[l]} = \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{[l]}} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ be the upstream gradient (already computed by the layer above or from the output). **Step 1 — Local gradient through activation (1.5 marks):** If the layer has an activation $g$, the pre-activation gradient is: $$ \delta^{[l]} \; \leftarrow \; \delta^{[l]} \odot g'(\mathbf{z}^{[l]}) $$ For ReLU: $g'(z) = \mathbf{1}[z > 0]$. **Step 2 — Gradient w.r.t. Weights (2 marks):** $$ \boxed{\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{[l]}} = \delta^{[l]} \, (\mathbf{a}^{[l-1]})^T} $$ For a mini-batch of size $B$, the gradient is averaged: $$ \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{[l]}} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{i=1}^{B} \delta^{i,[l]} (\mathbf{a}^{i,[l-1]})^T $$ **Step 3 — Gradient w.r.t. Biases (1.5 marks):** $$ \boxed{\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{[l]}} = \delta^{[l]}} $$ For a batch, sum over the batch dimension: $\sum_{i=1}^{B} \delta^{i,[l]}$. **Step 4 — Gradient w.r.t. Previous Activations (to backprop further) (2 marks):** $$ \boxed{\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{[l-1]}} = (\mathbf{W}^{[l]})^T \, \delta^{[l]}} $$ This is the critical step that propagates the error signal **backward** to the previous layer (or the flatten layer, or ultimately the conv layers via transposed convolutions / im2col). **Step 5 — Parameter update (optional, included for completeness):** Using gradient descent (or Adam, RMSprop, etc.): $$ \mathbf{W}^{[l]} \leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{[l]} - \alpha \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{[l]}}, \qquad \mathbf{b}^{[l]} \leftarrow \mathbf{b}^{[l]} - \alpha \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{[l]}} $$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting the activation function in backprop:** Many students compute $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}}$ as $\delta^{[l]} (\mathbf{a}^{[l-1]})^T$ directly without first applying $g'(\mathbf{z}^{[l]})$. The chain rule through the activation is **mandatory** for hidden layers. **(-2 marks typical)** > > 2. **Wrong shape in matrix multiplication:** Students often confuse $(\mathbf{a}^{[l-1]})^T \delta^{[l]}$ with $\delta^{[l]} (\mathbf{a}^{[l-1]})^T$. Remember: weight matrix shape is $(m, n)$, so the gradient must also be $(m, n)$. The correct order is **upstream gradient $\times$ input transpose**. **(-1.5 marks)** > > 3. **Skipping the flatten layer in diagrams:** Examiners deduct marks if the architecture diagram jumps from Pooling directly to FC without showing the Flatten. Always include it. > > 4. **Confusing Softmax with Sigmoid:** In a multi-class setting, the output is **Softmax over $K$ neurons**, NOT $K$ independent sigmoids. Independent sigmoids would not enforce the probability simplex constraint (sum to 1). **(-1 mark)** > > 5. **Forgetting the bias term in the forward equation:** Writing $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{W}\mathbf{a}$ without the $+\mathbf{b}$ is incomplete. **(-0.5 marks)** > > 6. **Not mentioning the chain rule in backprop:** Examiners expect you to explicitly state that you are using the chain rule, not just write the final formula. **(-1 mark)** --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fully Connected CNN** - A Fully Connected CNN = **Convolutional Base** (feature extractor) + **Flatten** + **Dense Head** (classifier). - The **Flatten** layer reshapes $(H_f, W_f, C_f) \rightarrow (H_f \cdot W_f \cdot C_f,)$; no parameters. - Forward pass of a Dense layer: $\mathbf{z}^{[l]} = \mathbf{W}^{[l]} \mathbf{a}^{[l-1]} + \mathbf{b}^{[l]}$; then $\mathbf{a}^{[l]} = g(\mathbf{z}^{[l]})$. - Hidden FC activations: **ReLU** (default). Output FC activation: **Softmax** (multi-class) or **Sigmoid** (binary). - Loss function: **Categorical Cross-Entropy** (multi-class) or **Binary Cross-Entropy** (binary). - The key gradient simplification: $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}_{\text{out}}} = \hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}$ (Softmax + CE combined). - Backprop formulas to memorize: - $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}} = \delta \, \mathbf{a}^T$ - $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}} = \delta$ - $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{a}_{\text{prev}}} = \mathbf{W}^T \delta$ - Apply $g'(\mathbf{z})$ element-wise before propagating $\delta$ further back. - **FC layers dominate parameter counts** — use **Dropout** ($p=0.5$), **L2 weight decay**, and consider **Global Average Pooling** as a modern alternative. - **Weight initialization matters:** Use **He initialization** for ReLU ($\sigma = \sqrt{2/n_{\text{in}}}$), **Xavier/Glorot** for Tanh/Sigmoid. - **Batch Normalization** can be inserted before the activation in FC layers to stabilize training. - The FC head can be **transferred** to new tasks: pre-trained conv base + new FC head = **Transfer Learning**. - **Modern trend:** Replace the FC head with a single linear layer after GAP for parameter efficiency (e.g., ResNet, MobileNet).
